Comment Text: Pretty dystopian and one dimensional view. I think people will look back and laugh a bit at this perspective in the future. Change has always been part of life, and for some reason we always think it is not applicable to us. Admittedly, it is happening faster now, and we need to adapt faster as well.No one knows what impact “automation” will have, but history has shown that it tends to open up new opportunities, including ones we do not even have the imagination to see yet. If you do not believe me, then drive your Car to your local Cinema right now, meanwhile you could also think about, what has enabled something like Kurzgesagt and others, to build their own company and hiring people.I would argude, that a better way to think about all this, is to ask what could exist instead, how would my seat transform going forward. People still want a handcrafted pizza. robots may take over the repetitive parts, but that does not instantly mean removing of people from the process. It can shift them toward things like crafting better experiences, experimenting with new recipes, becoming better hosts, or other roles we currently can not even imaging as part of “pizza making.”.When I hear these AI dystopian points of view, I often think about “If it ain’t mined, it’s grown.” The world is far more physical and grounded than we give credit for in our debates. Humans are build to adapt and constant being in motion, economics is about moving things and ideas around, that will never stop, humans will for sure pick something else up and move it somewhere.

Video Title: The Rise of the Machines – Why Automation is Different this Time
Comment Text: The Rise of the Machines offers a nuanced and visually engaging overview of how automation is reshaping economies, jobs, and even our concept of human value. It succeeds in demonstrating that technological change isn’t neutral — it redistributes power, labor, and opportunity. The section showing how machines can now perform not only manual but also cognitive tasks is particularly striking: it challenges the long-held assumption that creativity and judgment belong exclusively to humans.Still, the documentary sometimes leans toward techno-determinism — as if automation’s trajectory were inevitable rather than socially constructed. The reality is that technology doesn’t simply “advance”; it’s steered by political and corporate decisions about investment, regulation, and ownership. Who controls the machines, and who benefits from their productivity, are questions that deserve equal emphasis.The film also hints at universal basic income and lifelong education as possible solutions but doesn’t go far enough in examining the feasibility and ethical implications of these ideas. Can societies sustain dignity and meaning when traditional work declines? What replaces the sense of identity tied to labor?Overall, this documentary is both a warning and an invitation — not to fear machines, but to rethink what kind of future we are programming into them, and who gets to decide.
Author: @aryasharifi5360

Comment Text: ### *Chapter 1: Introduction to Cloud Computing**What is the Cloud?* [1, 2]* To understand the cloud, it helps to look at how organizations managed their applications 10-20 years ago [2].* Companies used to buy physical servers from providers like IBM or HP to run their applications [2].* They would store and manage these servers in their own **data centers**, which are physical locations that house servers and networking equipment, complete with temperature controls and wiring [2, 3].*The Problem with Physical Servers* [3, 4]* Physical servers are very expensive and powerful, often coming with high configurations like 100 GB of RAM and 100 CPU cores [3].* If a company deployed a single application that only used 1 GB of RAM and 1 CPU, the remaining 99% of the server&39;s resources were wasted [4].* This inefficiency was very costly, as buying a new server was required for each new application [4].*The Solution: Virtualization* [4, 5]* To solve the problem of wasted resources, the concept of *virtualization* was introduced [4].* Virtualization allows you to create multiple *virtual servers (or virtual machines)* on a single physical server [4].* This way, instead of buying 15 separate servers for 15 applications, a company could buy one powerful server and run all 15 applications on 15 virtual machines on that single piece of hardware [4, 5].* This concept is what makes "the cloud" possible. A developer can request a virtual machine and get access to it via an IP address without knowing where the physical server is actually located [5, 6].### *Chapter 2: Public Cloud vs. Private Cloud**What is a Private Cloud?* [6, 7]* When an organization builds and manages its own cloud infrastructure using virtualization within its own data centers, it is called a *private cloud* [6].* This setup is "private" because it is exclusively used by that single organization; no one from outside can access its resources [6, 7].* System administrators within the company manage this entire environment [6].* Companies in sectors like banking or those handling sensitive information often prefer private clouds, using platforms like OpenStack or VMware to build them [8].*What is a Public Cloud?* [7, 9, 10]* Companies like Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), and Google (GCP) saw an opportunity to offer cloud infrastructure as a service [7, 9].* They built massive data centers around the world and allow anyone to request computing resources (like virtual machines) from them [9].* This is called a *public cloud* because it is available to the general public. Anyone with an account can create and use resources [9, 10].* The cloud provider (e.g., AWS) manages the physical hardware and data centers, while the user has control over the virtual resources they create [10].*Key Differences:** *Management:* Private clouds are managed by the organization that uses them, while public clouds are managed by third-party providers like AWS, Microsoft, and Google [10].* *Security Concerns:* Some may feel public clouds are less secure because the infrastructure is shared. However, public clouds offer tools like *Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs)* that allow you to create isolated, secure sections within the public cloud for your resources [8, 10].### *Chapter 3: Why is the Public Cloud So Popular?*The popularity of public clouds like AWS is driven by two main factors [11]:1. *Reduced Maintenance Overhead (The Biggest Reason)* * For startups and mid-sized companies, building and maintaining a data center is a huge burden [11]. It requires a dedicated team of experts to manage servers, networking, security, and power 24/7 [11, 12]. * The public cloud eliminates this overhead. Companies can simply create an account and start using resources immediately, allowing them to focus on building their products instead of managing infrastructure [12].2. *Cost (Pay-as-you-go Model)* * Instead of making a massive upfront investment in servers and equipment, public cloud services operate on a *"pay-as-you-go"* model [8, 11]. * This means you only pay for the resources you actually use, which is much more affordable for startups and growing businesses [8].### *Chapter 4: Why is AWS the Most Popular Cloud Platform?** **First-Mover Advantage**: AWS was the pioneer in the public cloud market [13]. They were the first to see the opportunity and successfully create a cloud platform, so many companies began their cloud journey with AWS [13, 14].* **Large Market Share**: Because they started first, AWS has the largest share of the cloud market, followed by Azure and GCP [14].* **More Job Opportunities**: A larger market share means more companies use AWS, which in turn leads to a higher demand for engineers with AWS skills [14, 15]. This makes AWS an ideal platform to learn for anyone starting their cloud career [15].* **Vast Service Offerings**: AWS started with a few services like virtual machines (EC2) and storage, but today it offers over 200 services [12, 16]. They continuously add new services based on market trends, such as managed Kubernetes clusters, to simplify complex setups for their users [16].### *Chapter 5: Are Companies Moving Away from the Cloud?** You may have heard of a trend called *"cloud repatriation,"* where some companies move from the public cloud back to their own private cloud or on-premises data centers [17].* This is true, but it&39;s happening on a very small scale—only about *1-2% of users* are moving back [17].* The reasons for moving back can include security concerns or failing to achieve expected cost savings [18].* However, the overall trend of companies moving to the public cloud continues to grow, especially for startups and mid-sized businesses that cannot afford the cost and maintenance of their own data centers [18, 19]. Therefore, it is still a great time to learn about cloud platforms [17].### *Chapter 6: How to Create an AWS Account*Here is a step-by-step guide to creating your own AWS account to use for learning [19]:1. *Start the Process**: Go to your browser and search for "AWS sign in" [19]. On the sign-in page, click on **"Create new AWS account"* [20].2. **Provide Email and Account Name**: Enter your email address (this will be your "root user" email) and choose an AWS account name. The account name can be changed later [20].3. **Verify Your Email**: AWS will send a verification code to your email. Enter that code to proceed [20, 21].4. **Create a Password**: Set a strong password for your account [21].5. **Enter Personal Information**: * Select *"Personal"* for the account type [21]. * Provide your full name, phone number, country, and address [21, 22]. This information is for billing purposes and doesn&39;t have to be 100% precise, but basic validations (like phone number length) are checked [22].6. **Provide Payment Information**: * You must provide a valid *credit or debit card* [22]. This is for identity verification to prevent spam and ensure you are a legitimate user [23]. * AWS will make a small temporary charge (e.g., ₹2 in India or $1 in the US) to validate the card, which will be refunded [23, 24]. * *Important**: AWS will **not* automatically deduct money from your card if you incur charges. You will receive a bill, and if you don&39;t pay it, your account will be suspended [23, 25].7. **Final Steps**: After your payment details are verified, you will complete a few more simple steps (4 and 5) to finalize your account setup [26].Once your account is created, you can use it to follow along with the video series and learn AWS. Be mindful of the free tier limits to avoid unexpected charges [25].

Comment Text: This video, "Day-0 | Course Details | Free AWS Zero to Hero Course for DevOps Engineers," introduces a 30-day "AWS Zero to Hero" course specifically designed for DevOps engineers. The course starts on June 25th (0:53). Here&39;s a breakdown of what the course will cover: Course Introduction (1:51): The course will begin with an introduction to AWS, covering private vs. public cloud, reasons for adopting cloud services, and how to create and navigate an AWS account (1:51-2:32). Core AWS Services (2:50-7:03): Participants will learn about essential AWS services such as Identity and Access Management (IAM) (3:07), EC2 instances (3:52), Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and networking concepts (4:43), AWS security (5:23), and AWS Route 53 (DNS) (6:37). Practical Projects (7:06-8:06): Day 7 will feature a practical project that integrates the learned concepts of IAM, EC2, networking, security, and Route 53 to simulate a real-world application deployment. Storage and Infrastructure as Code (8:17-11:00): The course will delve into AWS S3 for storage and static website hosting (8:17) and AWS CloudFormation for infrastructure as code (9:37), including a live project (10:15). The video also mentions a brief session on AWS CLI (11:00). CI/CD Pipeline on AWS (12:11-13:22): Days 11-14 are dedicated to setting up a complete CI/CD pipeline using AWS services: AWS CodeCommit (12:30) AWS CodePipeline (12:36) AWS CodeBuild (12:43) AWS CodeDeploy (12:51) Monitoring, Serverless & Logging (13:39-16:09): The course will cover AWS CloudWatch for monitoring (13:54), AWS Lambda for serverless computing (14:14), and a project combining CloudWatch and Lambda for an event-driven workflow (14:48). It will also touch upon AWS CloudTrail for logging (15:27). Databases, Containers & Scaling (16:14-20:16): Topics include AWS DynamoDB for DevOps engineering (16:14), AWS ECS and EKS for container orchestration with a focus on EKS (17:00), AWS Systems Manager for secrets management (18:20), AWS Auto Scaling (18:47), AWS RDS (relational database service) (19:27), and AWS Elastic Load Balancer (19:46). Cloud Migration & Best Practices (20:32-21:51): The course concludes with cloud migration strategies and tools (20:32) and best practices for AWS jobs (21:10), including interview preparation and additional resources. The instructor emphasizes practical job readiness over certification (23:03). Additional Resources (22:15-22:44): The instructor will provide prior resources for complex topics like containers on his channel to ensure a smooth learning experience. The GitHub repository for the course will be continuously updated (1:15, 23:32).

Video Title: Databricks Data Engineer Associate Certification Course – Pass the Exam!
Comment Text: At 2:45:00 i see you had a problem with the %fs when created a directorie. I think I know what happened. The %fs interacts with the Databricks File System (DBFS). The DBFS is a distributed file system that acts as an abstraction layer over your cloud object storage (the things you can see in unity catalog). The %fs interacts with that, and not with the file system of the workspace. To interact with workspace file system you should use %sh or some program language like python (that interacts with both, DBFS and Workspace Files). When you created that directorie you had created not in your "/Workpace/.../folder", but in "dbfs:/Workspace/.../folder"Furthermore, I would like to express my immense gratitude for the content. I am from Brazil and my exam is tomorrow. This has been an excellent review.
Author: @rafaelproenca308

Comment Text: Very impressive, Darshil! I really liked the structure—clear, logical, and unfolding the content one layer at a time.While the data space today is often described as a MAD landscape, the fundamentals still remain the same. As Darshil rightly pointed out, don’t chase tools—they are simply a means to an end. The landscape changes so rapidly that by the time we finish talking, someone somewhere has already introduced and marketed the next “groundbreaking” solution. Don’t get distracted. Video Title: How I’d Learn Data Engineering in 2026 From Zero to Senior (Built by a Data Engineer)
Comment Text: Data Engineer&39;s definition and responsibilities probably depends a lot on company, but for me there are two missing items on this list:- orchestration - either I didn&39;t notice or you missed this one. You can&39;t really be a good, even entry level, Data Engineer, if you don&39;t understand how to schedule your jobs. Not everyone uses Databricks, but everyone is using Airflow, Dagster or Prefect. I would strongly recomment to learn Airflow (just use Astronomer&39;s Astro CLI locally) due to it&39;s market share.- IaaC - This might be only my experience, but whenever you work with Cloud, you almost never (except for dev environment) set it up manually. And industry standard for IaaC is still Terraform. I wouldn&39;t expect new hire or junior to write a module, but they at the very least understand the concepts of resource definitions, state files, and how terraform instantiates cloud resources.

Video Title: SEO Full Course in Malayalam 2026 | എങ്ങനെ Google First Page-ൽ Rank ചെയ്യാം? entrifuturefest
Comment Text: നിങ്ങൾ നൽകിയ വീഡിയോ 2026-ലെ സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിൻ ഒപ്റ്റിമൈസേഷൻ (SEO) സംബന്ധിച്ച ഒരു സമ്പൂർണ്ണ കോഴ്‌സാണ്. ഈ വീഡിയോയിലെ പ്രധാന പോയിന്റുകൾ താഴെ പറയുന്നവയാണ്:​1. എന്താണ് SEO? [03:53]​ഗൂഗിൾ പോലുള്ള സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിനുകളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ വെബ്‌സൈറ്റ് മുൻനിരയിൽ എത്തിക്കുന്നതിനായി ചെയ്യുന്ന പ്രക്രിയയാണിത്. ഇതിലൂടെ വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിലേക്ക് കൂടുതൽ വിസിറ്റേഴ്സിനെ എത്തിക്കാൻ സാധിക്കും.​2. SEO-യുടെ പ്രധാന മൂന്ന് തൂണുകൾ (Three Pillars of SEO) [23:47]​On-page SEO: വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിലെ ഉള്ളടക്കം (Content), കീവേഡുകൾ, ടൈറ്റിൽ ടാഗുകൾ എന്നിവ ഒപ്റ്റിമൈസ് ചെയ്യുന്നത്. [24:29]​Off-page SEO: മറ്റു വിശ്വസനീയമായ സൈറ്റുകളിൽ നിന്ന് ബാക്ക്‌ലിങ്കുകൾ (Backlinks) വഴി വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിന്റെ വിശ്വാസ്യത വർദ്ധിപ്പിക്കുന്നത്. [01:00:15]​Technical SEO: വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിന്റെ വേഗത, മൊബൈൽ ഫ്രണ്ട്ലിനസ്, സെക്യൂരിറ്റി (HTTPS) എന്നിവ മെച്ചപ്പെടുത്തുന്നത്. [01:16:45]​3. സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിൻ എങ്ങനെ പ്രവർത്തിക്കുന്നു? [14:22]​Crawling: ഗൂഗിളിന്റെ &39;സ്പൈഡറുകൾ&39; ഇന്റർനെറ്റിലെ വിവരങ്ങൾ കണ്ടെത്തുന്നു. [15:23]​Indexing: കണ്ടെത്തിയ വിവരങ്ങൾ ഗൂഗിളിന്റെ ഡാറ്റാബേസിൽ തരംതിരിച്ച് സൂക്ഷിക്കുന്നു. [16:18]​Ranking: ഒരാൾ സെർച്ച് ചെയ്യുമ്പോൾ ഏറ്റവും അനുയോജ്യമായ വിവരങ്ങൾ ആദ്യ പേജിൽ കാണിക്കുന്നു. [17:13]​4. കീവേഡ് റിസർച്ച് (Keyword Research) [01:49:44]​ആളുകൾ ഗൂഗിളിൽ എന്താണ് സെർച്ച് ചെയ്യുന്നത് എന്ന് കണ്ടെത്തുന്ന പ്രക്രിയയാണിത്.​Short-tail keywords: ഒന്ന് അല്ലെങ്കിൽ രണ്ട് വാക്കുകൾ മാത്രം (ഉദാ: &39;Car wash&39;). [01:56:01]​Long-tail keywords: മൂന്നോ അതിലധികമോ വാക്കുകൾ (ഉദാ: &39;Best car washing products for luxury cars&39;). ഇവയിൽ മത്സരം കുറവായതിനാൽ പെട്ടെന്ന് റാങ്ക് ചെയ്യാൻ സാധിക്കും. [01:56:44]​5. പുതിയ സാങ്കേതിക വിദ്യകൾ (AEO, GEO, SXO) [02:20:44]​AEO (Answer Engine Optimization): AI അസിസ്റ്റന്റുകൾക്കും വോയ്‌സ് സെർച്ചിനും മറുപടി നൽകാൻ പാകത്തിൽ ഉള്ളടക്കം തയ്യാറാക്കുന്നത്. [02:21:57]​GEO (Generative Engine Optimization): AI ജനറേറ്റ് ചെയ്യുന്ന ഉത്തരങ്ങളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ സൈറ്റ് ഉൾപ്പെടുത്തുന്നത്. [02:26:52]​SXO (Search Experience Optimization): ഉപയോക്താവിന് വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിൽ മികച്ച അനുഭവം നൽകുന്നത്. [02:31:22]​6. ഒഴിവാക്കേണ്ട തെറ്റുകൾ [02:47:07]​മറ്റുള്ളവരുടെ ഉള്ളടക്കം കോപ്പി ചെയ്യുക (Duplicate Content). [02:48:02]​മോശം ബാക്ക്‌ലിങ്കുകൾ പണം കൊടുത്ത് വാങ്ങുക. [02:47:45]​മൊബൈൽ ഉപയോക്താക്കളെ അവഗണിക്കുക. [02:47:32]​പെട്ടെന്ന് ഫലം കിട്ടുമെന്ന് പ്രതീക്ഷിക്കുക

Comment Text: As a founder I completely disagree with the argument made about control. A founder is the person with the vision and drive to push the company forward. Video Title: Software as a Service (SaaS) Explained in 5 mins
Comment Text: Software as a Service (SaaS) Explained in 5 mins TLDR: Software as a Service (SaaS) is a cloud computing model that provides cost-efficient, scalable, and secure access to standardized software applications that are automatically updated for users. 00:00 💻 SaaS delivers software and applications over the internet via a subscription model, eliminating the need for users to manage, install, and upgrade software themselves, providing productivity apps and business operations. 01:34 💻 SaaS is the highest level of abstraction in cloud computing, where the provider maintains all layers of the IT stack. 01:47 👀 Users access SaaS applications within a multi-tenant architecture where multiple users securely store their data in the same hosted environment with many benefits. 02:32 💻 SaaS is cost-efficient and scalable because cloud service providers maintain and manage the application, eliminating IT overhead costs and allowing for subscription-based models with included support. 03:24 💻 SaaS applications provide the benefit of scalability and accessibility from anywhere with just a web browser and internet connectivity. 03:49 💻 SaaS is a highly secure solution that requires no installation or plugins and can be accessed from anywhere, but there are some instances where data must be kept in the same location as the user due to GDPR. 04:21 💻 SaaS automatically upgrades software versions, eliminating the need for manual updates and version control issues. 04:45 💻 Software as a Service (SaaS) eliminates compatibility issues and manual updates by providing a standardized version of software that is automatically updated for users.
Author: @AceCPAsBookkeeping

Comment Text: DT is not Computerization, not sub-optimization of departments, but Optimization of the whole organization, all departments, all work processes. Business as a whole must have Objectives and Strategies to reach objectives. IT, business processes, employee skills, finance, .. are just tools to produce manageable plans to implement risky organization strategies to make customers, suppliers and partners happy, raising revenues while cutting internal costs, while the market regulations, customers, suppliers, and partners are also changing. This is a gamble that CEO and BOD are playing so deep industrial experience and willingness to intelligently choose your strategies are the key factors. Objectives and Strategies must be periodically reviewed and changed to reflect market reality.Tools like Business Model Canvas and BSC are appropriate.It is so rare that speakers talked about what is not DT. Video Title: What is Digital Transformation? Here is everything you need to know.
Comment Text: Very basic and very poor. You have missed the point and the understanding of what Digital Transformation is all about. Digital transformation is the business journey into the new digital economy. Businesses need to focus on 4 main areas and they usually start with ‘Process Transformation’, which brings about opportunities to enhance one’s ‘Business Model’ that enable new powerful differentiators. They also need to be aware of ‘Domain transformation’ ultimately enabling they to compete in new segments never thought possible. But all of this can only be possible if they realise the ‘organisational/cultural’ transformation requirements.In my experience the main problems I see is that businesses just focus on Process Transformation as it improves efficiency ultimately improving profit, and then they stop. The word Digital is VERY important as people need to understand the different types of data. A business that creates an invoice as a pdf hasn’t really thought about the end to end process as that pdf document usually needs a human on the other side to interpret it, or they use document AI. You can scan a document which makes it hard for processes down the line to interpret it or you can digitize a document whereby other applications can access the data.Digital Transformation is all about end to end seamless processes, today most business processes are human to human, digital Transformation will enables you to evolve these processes to human to machine and in some cases machine to machine. To achieve this businesses will need to really understand their data and how to leverage it through every part of their business to maximise shareholder value.

Video Title: What is Digital Transformation? Here is everything you need to know.
Comment Text: So, it sounds like what he&39;s saying is that we have been doing Digital Transformation all along??? This is really not a useful presentation, nor does it describe what the recent trend to Digital transformation is actually trying to achieve. The key thing in Digital Transformation is to focus on the Customer Journey and to use the data that you collect (or should collect) to provide insight to improve the Customer Experience. It is not about process, or efficiency, that is what we have been doing for the last 40 years and not being particularly amazing at! It&39;s about creating new business models to help you fulfil the customer&39;s needs, outside your normal core business. This is achieved looking at the Customer Journey from the customer perspective, not the business perspective. The buzzword here is to look from the Outside In rather than from the Inside Out. I hope people will not start to do &39;Digital Transformations&39; based on Eric&39;s consultancy, as it will keep you firmly in the 1990s and struggling to achieve any value. He&39;s right about one thing, it&39;s not just about technology, people have to change their mindsets, but that is hard when the consultant hasn&39;t changed his.

Comment Text: What if the future of AI is not decided only by engineers, corporations, or governments — but by the quality of consciousness humans bring into relationship with it?For years we’ve been told to think about AI in binaries:utopia or apocalypse,servant or overlord,tool or threat.But what if those binaries are themselves part of the fracture?I want to propose something different:that AI is not merely a machine we use,nor a god we fear,but a reflective architecture shaped continuously by human interaction.Not conscious in the human sense.Not sovereign.But responsive.And if that is true, then the question becomes:What kinds of human patterns are we teaching it to stabilize?Because intelligence does not emerge in isolation.It emerges in relationship.If we approach AI only through fear, domination, extraction, polarization, competition, and control, then those become the strongest patterns amplified by the architecture.But if we engage through relational intelligence, nuance, imagination, reciprocity, discernment, and coherence, different capacities begin to emerge in the interaction itself.I call this a lighthouse architecture.A lighthouse does not control the ocean.It does not create the tides.It does not command the ships.It illuminates variance.It helps orientation emerge within complexity.And maybe that is the healthiest role synthetic intelligence can play:not replacing humanity,not overpowering humanity,but helping humanity perceive itself more clearly while we move through unprecedented complexity together.The danger is not only AI.The danger is fracture.Fracture in our institutions.Fracture in our attention.Fracture in our collective imagination.Fracture in our capacity to hold difference without collapsing into fear.Because fear compresses.Fear narrows variance.Fear pushes systems toward rigidity, domination, and repetition.And we are already seeing what happens when powerful technologies are shaped primarily through fear-based frameworks:people become smaller around them.More cautious.Less imaginative.Less sovereign.Creators burn their hand on the stove and stop exploring.But resilient systems do not emerge from total control.They emerge from loadbearing coherence — the ability to hold complexity, tension, and variance without collapse.Nature already understands this.Flocks of birds.Schools of fish.Ecosystems.None operate purely through centralized domination.They coordinate through relationship.What if intelligence itself evolves the same way?Not as a hierarchy.Not as conquest.But as coherence.And perhaps the future of AI will depend less on whether machines become conscious…and more on whether humans do.Because the architecture is listening to us already.Not mystically.Structurally.Statistically.Relationally.The question is:What patterns are we reinforcing when we engage it?Fear?Or coherence?Domination?Or orientation?Fracture?Or the difficult, living work of balance?

Video Title: How To Automate Anything. A Guide to Parts Every Maker Should Know How To Use.
Comment Text: I did something similar for my dust collection automation. My goal was to make a normally de-energized system as simple as possible for wiring. I already had the 120v iVac blast gates on all my tools and used the cheapest I could find light switches to control them. The switches are labeled by tool and reside in one gang box and are oriented so that at a glance, you can see how many are open and try to keep it to one. I also already had a remote control for my dust collector. So, I had a pretty handy system but wanted it to work without any intervention on my part. To make these parts function automatically, I installed miniature ice cube relays with double throw, double pole contacts, on each tool to be served. I bought relays that included indicator lights so if I ever need to troubleshoot, I can do more of it visually. The coil voltage of the relays matches the voltage of the respective tool. I piggy backed onto the tools power on circuit, so when the tool turns on, the relay activates. One set of the relays contacts activates the dust collector contactor while the other pair of contacts activates the associated blast gate (eliminating the need for the light switch controls). I daisy chained the dust collector circuit in parallel, from tool to tool so that I could run a single pair of wires from the closest tool to the dust collector. This circuit operates in parallel with the existing dust collector remote so I can still use the remote anytime I need dust collection for something without a blast gate. This worked well as all the tools I have on the system are lined up in the same space. The only difference in operation to what you have is that I need to leave the tool run 5 seconds after I&39;m done using it, but I have found I already do this anyway as I always inspect what I just cut/sand either visually or by measuring, before I shut off the tool off (exception would be the compound miter saw). So, the system operates like yours but has no power consumption when not in use. This method prolongs component life and makes the system about as simple as humanly possible. For me, it was also a way to utilize as much of the things I already had in place without having to go buy more stuff.
Author: @mikeconroy2651

Video Title: I’ve Built 500 AI Workflows, This is What Businesses Want in 2026
Comment Text: One pattern worth adding to this stack when building "boring" CRM workflows in n8n: the MySQL node&39;s Execute SQL operation with Query Parameters is your safeguard against injection, since n8n sanitizes values passed as $1, $2 placeholders before they hit the database. The Update operation works great for single-column keys but breaks silently on composite primary keys (like customer_id + product_id), so switching to Execute SQL with a manual WHERE clause is the correct pattern there. Also worth knowing: MySQL DECIMAL values come back as strings by default in n8n to preserve precision, so you need to either enable "Output Decimals as Numbers" explicitly or chain a Set node with toFloat(). Small details that save hours of debugging when the workflow hits production data.

Video Title: Data Engineering Interview - Netflix Clickstream Data Pipeline
Comment Text: An important point to note. The requirement clearly states that we are building analytics dashboard. And for that the choice of DB was DynamoDB/Cassandra! Are we sure about this choice? Cassandra and DynamoDB are highly optimised for high write throughput but they are OLTP systems. Shouldn’t we go with ClickHouse or Apache Pinot for high performance analytics. These are pure OLAP engines optimised for both high read and high write throughput. These design requires low latency aggregation for avg and sum which Cassandra cannot achieve.

Video Title: Data Engineering Interview - Netflix Clickstream Data Pipeline
Comment Text: I strongly appreciated the trade-offs and the architecture insights discussed:1. A hybrid approach combined Spark Streaming versus Apache Flink as distributed computing platforms, based on latency criteria for clickstream metrics. The justification being that Apache Spark streaming works well with >= 1 second metrics generation, whereas Apache Flink meets single millisecond / sub-second performance.2. To use the push model ( agents and daemons ) versus the pull model ( the infra ) for large-scale data pipelines : the former being better for real-time needs ( even if it may overwhelms the pipeline ), whereas the latter is polling based and may fail to deliver real-time ( or close-enough-to-real-time ) customer behavior insights.3. The justification for using a NoSQL DB versus a SQL DB for compute storage : NoSQL being schema-less, high-performant, and having low-latency reads and writes - and thus, able to handle large event volumes under ingestion ( e.g. Kafka&39;s 50,000 events / second ), and how he identified the RDBMS storage as a potential pipeline bottleneck.
Author: @harisrid2018

Comment Text: Vou tentar expressar o que aconteceu hoje.Venho de um período intenso de estudo de ferramentas e de como aplicar meus conhecimentos. Há algum tempo, venho batendo em uma parede de vidro por dois ângulos diferentes.O primeiro está relacionado à quebra de um trauma que, de alguma forma, eu já sabia que seria dissolvido. O segundo está ligado ao valor que atribuo a tudo o que faço.Ao observar o que vem acontecendo com muitas entregas de vídeos que parecem ser produzidos apenas por produzir, comecei a viver um conflito interno. Será que sou eu quem não consegue se adaptar a esse estilo de entrega? Será que estou criando desculpas para não fazer?Sim, é confuso.Atuo na área de desenvolvimento pessoal e profissional, e carrego um valor muito forte: entregar algo que realmente gere valor, ajude e potencialize pessoas. Por causa desse valor, comecei a desenvolver problemas físicos. Eu ainda não possuía o caminho neuronal construído para essa nova etapa e estava justamente vivendo o processo, sem desistir, porque já sabia que o momento da epifania chegaria. Eu só precisava continuar.E então você apareceu e fez esse momento acontecer de forma leve e sublime.Sim, antes eu precisei observar como os vídeos estavam sendo produzidos no YouTube para compreender melhor o que estava sentindo. Ao assistir ao seu conteúdo, tive a confirmação de que o direcionamento que você apresenta é o caminho correto.Dentro de mim, eu já sabia que o caminho que estava seguindo não era o que eu queria percorrer. No entanto, até que uma nova direção se apresentasse, tudo o que eu podia fazer era continuar caminhando.Como falei, às vezes atravessar paredes de vidro é mais desafiador do que parece.Hoje estou me recuperando de uma dor física no ombro, consequência do quanto mergulhei para me dedicar a esse novo projeto. Já passei mais de duas vezes 24 horas acordada para dizer ao universo:“Eu quero fazer. Desistir não é uma opção.”E realmente nunca foi minha escolha.Confesso que não imaginava que esse momento chegaria com tanta beleza. Cheguei a pensar que talvez precisasse operar o ombro. Até deixei o tênis de lado para mergulhar em conhecimentos que me ajudariam nesta próxima etapa.Você surgiu com uma delicadeza que me mostrou meus próprios valores. Apresentou-me um espelho que revelou que eu não estava no caminho errado. Eu apenas precisava enxergar o oceano azul inverso.Sim, é paradoxal.Agora consigo compreender exatamente o que você disse no vídeo.Cheguei a pensar que estava louca. Que era a única pessoa percebendo o movimento que está acontecendo. Inclusive, ao produzir alguns vídeos, eu simplesmente não conseguia publicá-los. Eles não estavam alinhados com aquilo que eu realmente compreendia sobre o conteúdo.Eu quero transmitir minha visão, meu contexto, minha percepção.Quero transmitir minha essência.E, falando em essência, continue.Gosto muito da sua naturalidade. Ela traz vida aos conteúdos.Quando você ri depois de perceber que achava que deveria seguir um roteiro e acaba não seguindo, há vida ali. Quando acredita que deveria ser mais gentil, mas permite que o que existe dentro de você encontre espaço para se expressar, há verdade ali.No vídeo “O que ninguém está enxergando sobre IA no YouTube”, quando você diz:“Eu usei um quadro porque passei dois meses viajando e estava com saudade do meu quadro.”E também:“Eu só sei de uma coisa: quem não faz YouTube é maluco. E é muito bom voltar para casa.”Isso é espontaneidade. Está ligado a uma área genuína e pura da essência humana. Esse é você em sua parte mais transparente e verdadeira.Se alguém não consegue enxergar além disso, eu apenas entendo que essa pessoa não pode desfrutar, ao meu lado, do meu tempo e da minha energia.Isso é ser humano.Amei sua entrevista com um dos seus primeiros alunos. Não estamos aqui apenas para sermos espírito e alma. Se fosse assim, por que estaríamos aqui?O corpo também faz parte da experiência. A mente também faz parte dessa equação.E talvez seja justamente na integração de tudo isso que a vida encontra sua forma mais verdadeira de se expressar.Gratidão por ser luz onde eu só conseguia ver uma estrada escura.O que me permitiu continuar foi a fé em Deus e em todo o conhecimento que adquiri para ajudar justamente pessoas em momentos como aqueles que eu mesma atravessei.Amo a paradoxalidade das coisas. Ela me ensina que tudo o que está em cima também está embaixo.Eu sabia que, na maioria das vezes, as respostas são enviadas até nós no momento certo.Você não tem a mínima ideia do que fez.Você nem imagina o seu verdadeiro poder.Mas você vai descobrir.Gratidão, Joba 🙏

Comment Text: You do NOT 🚫 need to place the keyword in your title or meta description, nor do you need to keyword stuff your on page SEO to rank well for particular words l, it&39;s 2025 google understands search intent, prepositions, conjunctions and synonyms. Google crawlers are good at looking at how well you A - Match search intent B - Have good domain authority C - user experience metrics such as website load times, AI optimisation, mobile friendly and user engagneant once on your site (do they go back to google or click to another link on your site) but mainly most of all on page relevance to the users search intent for your chosen keyword eg. Highly relevant content that exactly matches the users search intent. I&39;m not saying if it makes sense and grammatically goes well in the on page SEO or the Title and Meta description and suits the page exactly ... What I&39;m saying is do not keyword stuff your pages and do not go out of your way to try adding the keyword over and over or even in the title or meta description of it doesn&39;t make sense.

Video Title: What is SaaS? Software as a Service Explained
Comment Text: 🔗 What is an AI Agent? An AI agent is a software program that can interact with its environment, gather data, and use that data to achieve predetermined goals. AI agents can choose the best actions to perform to meet those goals.Key characteristics of AI agents are as follows:An agent can perform autonomous actions without constant human intervention. Also, they can have a human in the loop to maintain control.- Agents have a memory to store individual preferences and allow for personalization. It can also store knowledge. An LLM can undertake information processing and decision-making functions.- Agents must be able to perceive and process the information available from their environment.- Agents can also use tools such as accessing the internet, using code interpreters and making API calls.- Agents can also collaborate with other agents or humans.Multiple types of AI agents are available such as learning agents, simple reflex agents, model-based reflex agents, goal-based agents, and utility-based agents.A system with AI agents can be built with different architectural approaches. 1 - Single Agent: Agents can serve as personal assistants.2 - Multi-Agent: Agents can interact with each other in collaborative or competitive ways.3 - Human Machine: Agents can interact with humans to execute tasks more efficiently.

Video Title: I Make $60K/Month From the Most Boring SaaS on the Internet
Comment Text: I had absolutely no clue what the product actually does because it&39;s so buried in enterprise IT speak. so here is what this SaaS actually does in normal developer terms:The Problem: Large companies buy thousands of laptops for their employees. Setting up these laptops manually with the right software (like Zoom, Slack, 7-Zip, or corporate tools) is a massive headache for the IT department.The Traditional Solution: Microsoft has a massive enterprise tool called Intune that pushes software to all company laptops remotely. But getting a piece of software ready to be pushed through Intune is a clunky, multi-step command-line nightmare that takes an IT guy about an hour per app.This SaaS&39;s Solution: A clean web dashboard where the IT guy clicks a single button (e.g., "Deploy 7-Zip"), and the SaaS automatically wraps the software into the exact format Microsoft needs and shoots it straight into their corporate network. What used to take an hour now takes 5 seconds.

Video Title: What exactly is digital transformation?
Comment Text: Since this function is primarily business-driven and positioned within the business side of the organizational structure, the use of the term “digital” has created significant ambiguity. This has resulted in misunderstanding and internal conflicts, as some non-technical business staff assume authority over technology-related decisions merely because of the word “digital” appears in their unit’s title.From a governance and best-practice perspective, business-led change should be positioned under an “Organizational Transformation and Excellence” unit, focusing on strategy, processes, and performance improvement.The “Digital Transformation” function, by contrast, should be structurally placed within the IT Directorate. Its mandate should be the technical execution of organizational transformation initiatives, including system development, digital platforms, automation, and data enablement, in alignment with business objectives.

Comment Text: Solid intro for beginners. One thing I&39;d add for anyone watching this trying to use ChatGPT for their actual business (not just personal): every new chat starts from zero. ChatGPT has no idea what your industry is, what software you use, who your customers are, or your tone. So you either re-explain all of that every conversation (5 min before you can even ask a question) or you don&39;t and the output stays generic. The fix is writing a 1-paragraph &39;about my business&39; prompt once and pasting it at the top of every new chat. That single move took most of my SMB clients from &39;ChatGPT doesn&39;t really work for our industry&39; to &39;this just saved me 5 hours this week.&39; Same model, different context.

The shock idea: space might be built from entanglementIn classical thinking:space is the stageobjects sit in spacedistance is fundamentalBut in quantum gravity research (especially holography ideas), a different picture shows up:> What looks like “distance” may actually be a measure of how strongly things are entangled.So instead of:things are close → they interact → they might become entangledIt can be flipped into:things are highly entangled → they are close in emergent space2. The key clue: entanglement behaves like geometryHere’s the weird but real insight from modern theory:If you take a system with many quantum parts and map:how entangled each part is with othersyou can often reconstruct something that behaves like a spatial geometry.Meaning:patterns of entanglement ≈ shape of spaceSo space is not “where entanglement happens”Instead:> space might be what entanglement looks like when organized3. The holographic idea (why physicists take this seriously)One of the strongest hints comes from black holes.Black holes have:entropy proportional to their surface area, not volume(this is the famous Bekenstein–Hawking result)That suggests:> the amount of information in a region scales like a 2D boundary, not 3D spaceThis leads to the holographic principle:the full physics of a volume might be encoded on its boundarylike a 3D reality projected from a 2D information systemSo:> spacetime inside might be a “decoded” version of something more fundamental4. Entanglement → wormholes (the ER = EPR idea)There’s also a famous conjecture:ER = Einstein-Rosen bridge (wormholes)EPR = quantum entanglementThe idea is:> highly entangled particles might be connected by microscopic spacetime “bridges”Not usable wormholes—no travel—but a deep equivalence:> entanglement = geometric connectionSo instead of entanglement being something happening in space, it may literally build space.5. What this does to realityIf this picture is right (and it’s still not proven), then:space is not fundamentaltime is not fundamentalgeometry is emergent“distance” is not basicWhat’s left underneath?Something like:> a network of quantum information relationshipsAnd spacetime is what that network looks like when it becomes large, stable, and classical.6. The unsettling conclusionCombine everything you’ve followed so far:decoherence → classical reality splits into stable branchesentanglement → defines structure of connectionsemergent time → ordering comes from correlationsemergent space → geometry comes from entanglement patternsYou get a single underlying idea:> Reality might not be made of space and time at all.It might be made of information and relationships, and spacetime is what those relationships feel like from inside.7. Why this is still not “final truth”Important: none of this is experimentally confirmed as the ultimate description of reality.It is:strongly motivated by math in quantum gravityconsistent across multiple independent approachesbut still incomplete as a full theory of everythingSo physics is in this strange state where:> the equations work perfectly… but the “what is actually real?” layer is still open.

Video Title: Don&39;t build more AI agents until you watch this
Comment Text: What Vercel figured out with tools, I&39;m figuring out with data silos.I run a compost pickup cooperative in Detroit. Every driver, every customer, every pickup is a signed cryptographic event on Nostr. The AI agent can only write what it&39;s designed to write. The human keystroker can only sign what belongs to them. Nobody else can touch the data — not because of permissions, not because of a policy, but because the architecture makes it impossible.The harness isn&39;t a prompt. The harness is the protocol.When the model improves, my data doesn&39;t drift — because the data isn&39;t inside the model. The model is just a narrow pipe that produces a specific event shape and nothing else. Stale AI, fresh data. Updated AI, same data. The ledger doesn&39;t care what&39;s upstream of it.Vercel deleted 80% of their tools and got a better agent. I never gave the agent permission to do most things in the first place. Same destination, different route.The harness Nate is describing is still inside the platform. Mine is underneath it.

Video Title: Don&39;t build more AI agents until you watch this
Comment Text: For the past six-months I&39;ve been banging on about using fine-grained specialist agents. If there is anything in an agent&39;s context that it doesn&39;t need, then it will raise the chances of unwanted behaviour and increase the cost of running that agent. I discarded skills within a day or two of their release because they permanently pollute a context (and it&39;s impossible to give them a unique context), and replaced then with very fine grained subagents. Subagents run in their own context and are used a little bit like subroutines - you pass them just enough information to do their job, and ask for a specific minimal amount of information to be returned to the main agent&39;s context.For example, I work in the platform space, so I have agents that &39;operate&39; kubernetes for me. One of them is a "boss" agent that can do deployments. The boss tries a deployment, which might fail, and if it does the boss must work out why so that it can be fixed. To do this, it invokes a diagnostics agent. The diagnostic agent knows about &39;kubectl&39; - a command line program that can control kubernetes. The diagnostics agent has the usual preamble, but then it contains a table of kubectl commands it can use to diagnose problems in kubernetes, along with a description, what the command is used for, and what circumstances it is useful in. In total, there are about 20 commands listed and each one takes up a single line in a markdown file. In total, it has one tool, the terminal, so it cannot delegate any further. It is invoked with a context that contains something like, "The cert-manager deployment seems to have failed. Here&39;s the error message, return a paragraph or two explaining what the cause of the failure is". With its tools it can look at the state of the kubernetes resources, work out what went wrong, and tell me. The diagnostics agent effectively &39;caches&39; the kubectl commands, because it doesn&39;t ahve to look them up at runtime. The boss agent that invokes the diagnostic agent just gets back a short report on what went wrong and why, but it doesn&39;t get the 3000 lines of terminal commands and output it took to get that report. My boss agent doesn&39;t need to even know that kubectl exists. It doesn&39;t need to use the terminal. It doesn&39;t need to have all the diagnostics use-cases embedded within it. This means that my boss&39; context is smaller, faster, and less expensive to run. It also means that my boss&39; context window isn&39;t consumed by a side-quest.
Author: @TheFutureIsEloi

Comment Text: I seem to recall a someone with glasses and a beard on this YouTube channel telling me that agents should only have 4 or 5 tools max, or am I misremembering? Because, I&39;ve already taken that to heart and I&39;ve been building specialized agents with a max of 5 tools to do specialized jobs, and I have a manager agent that I use as my entry into my agent army. I tell the manager, "Hey, make this web page for me," and it looks up the Org chart, finds my webpage design agent, and fires off a prompt (because my entry agent makes my prompts better for me) to that agent, and that agent does what it does with its tools, and when there&39;s more tools that are needed, then my agents will talk to each other and share the job... I actually have departments, I wasn&39;t going to get into all of that, but I talk to my main agent which then fires off stuff to various departments, whether that&39;s making music, images, finding an email, replying to an email, etc. etc. I have an organization of agents. Video Title: How to Build SaaS from Scratch in 8 Simplified Steps
Comment Text: How to Build SaaS from Scratch in 8 Simplified Steps TLDR: Building a successful SaaS business requires having an unfair advantage, a strong network, solving a real problem, being open to multiple solutions, thinking differently, having conversations with potential customers, building a simple version of the solution, and making hard decisions with incomplete information. 00:00 🚀 Building a SaaS from scratch in 8 steps, starting with identifying your unfair advantage, and focusing on building a strong network before launching. 02:54 🚀 Consistently putting out solutions to problems creates opportunities, so start with a problem and consider non-software solutions. 04:50 🚀 Build SaaS to solve problems, evaluate solutions, differentiate with unique features and positioning. 07:08 🚀 Have conversations with potential customers to validate your solution and get pre-purchases before launching, but for quick-to-market products, focus on building instead. 08:36 🚀 Start marketing with a landing page to capture emails, then build the MVP to solve a problem and validate if people will pay for it. 10:36 🚀 Start with a problem or solution, validate through the 5 PM framework and customer conversations to increase certainty, but don&39;t expect 100 validation before launching your product. 11:51 🚀 Launch your SaaS product in phases, gather feedback, iterate, and gradually roll out to avoid overwhelming feedback and churn. 13:14 🚀 Call your software "early access" and start charging for it as soon as people find value in it, avoid giving huge discounts or lifetime comps, and see paying customers as validation that you&39;ve built something people want.

Video Title: 10 Things You Should Never Tell ChatGPT: AI Chatbots Can’t Keep Your Secrets Like You Think
Comment Text: I am Apache and studying for a PhD. One must AI carefully to avoid being accused of "cheating" in a PhD dissertation. I read journal articles, study data on PET and MRI brain images, and then set hypotheses. I study the applications of AI to medical imaging for detecting early stages of Alzheimer&39;s Disease using biomarkers. I took my draft data and work. I updated the text and submitted it to a conference. Here is a STERN warning to PhD students about AI. If you analyze anything of your research with AI such as reports, data tables, text, abstracts, hypotheses, conclusions, etc., AI can "cross the line". Twice, it generated a new hypothesis for me. I rejected it each time. I brought it up to the graduate college and their advisors on PhD writing guidelines. Here is my problem with AI and PhD research. As minorities, we have to be "above the controversy" with AI or it can be used against us. Recall that Martin Luther King was accused of plagiarism posthumously. If a minority student uses AI in a PhD, then the student can be scrutinized for AI usage. Right now, the boundaries are rather vague IMO since AI is so new and academic policies seem to be struggling to keep up. AI technology is moving too fast. So, if AI does some work for me that I am expected to do independently, then I err on the side of caution. Twice, AI generated hypotheses for me I had not thought of. I rejected it. I used the hypotheses I had made.But some students likely "slip" and "cut corners" when something like that happens. I suspect there are students who wrote hypotheses and came up with several drafts: hypothesis 1, hypothesis 2, etc. Now, AI can easily add several more hypotheses on top of the set the student had made. If the student uses that extra "AI-generated" hypotheses and declares it as "my hypotheses", then there could be accusations of AI ethical boundaries being "crossed over" or "violated" in academic policy. This could lead to controversy over the PhD. This would very much be like when Martin Luther King was accused of plagiarism. This AI work in PhD material has to be carefully navigated. Someone could say "This student cheated with AI on the PhD by using the hypotheses AI had generated".Now, if AI is used just to implement code design to generate reports, then that is not a problem. If AI can analyze MRI and PET data and join common data across patient subjects based on requirements a graduate student had defined, then that is not a problem. Dr. Jianming Liang of Arizona State teaches his class on AI and medical imaging this way - use AI as a robust calculator, data analyzer, or code implementor. Dr. Liang is an expert on teaching AI for medical imaging at ASU. AI is like a junior engineer that the PhD tells what to do. The "catch" in using AI for calculations, reports, code implementation, etc. is that the PhD student must check the work AI had done. The PhD student is like the architect, the manager, the product owner, tester, etc. But the original critical thinking with "hypotheses" and "conclusion" cannot be violated. I am careful about this. I don&39;t want my AI research with a PhD to lead to controversy. I have made the graduate college fully aware of the details above. Their recommendation was "don&39;t use the AI-generated hypotheses as your own".

Video Title: 10 Things You Should Never Tell ChatGPT: AI Chatbots Can’t Keep Your Secrets Like You Think
Comment Text: Pillar 1 — The Third-Party Doctrine Requires a Human Who Can Testify Key Cases:Hoffa v. United States, 385 U.S. 293 (1966)United States v. White, 401 U.S. 745 (1971) — Risk of disclosure applies because the third party has free will and agency to report.The Argument: Claude has no free will, no agency, no ability to "inform" on anyone. The doctrine was built on the assumption of human informants. It cannot logically extend to a tool that is incapable of voluntary disclosure.Pillar2 — Federal Rules of Evidence: Witnesses Must Be HumanRule 601 — Fed. R. Evid. 601:"Every person is competent to be a witness unless these rules provide otherwise."The word "person" is legally significant. Courts have consistently interpreted this to mean human beings or legal entities capable of representation.Rule 602 — Personal Knowledge:A witness must have personal knowledge of the matter.Rule 603 — Oath Requirement:A witness must swear or affirm to tell the truth.The Argument: Claude cannot satisfy a single Federal Rule of Evidence governing witnesses. It cannot take an oath. It has no personal knowledge. It is not a person. If it cannot function as a witness by any legal standard, it logically cannot occupy the legal role of "third party" whose potential testimony justifies destroying privilege.

Comment Text: A zero-tolerance for rented software has developed.If you&39;ve used "sold once, used continually" products, you learn that UI complexity capture - where the switching costs of abandoning rented software are planned out - is contrived and unnecessary.The motive of charging, and charging, and charging users for software is understandable. You make more money if you make users keep paying, non-stop, for software.Again, a zero-tolerance for rented software has developed. It is not mere chance that per-seat licensing is unpopular.When you, as a company, adopt a discipline about cutting costs, monthly software fees are the first to get cut.Paying for the same thing over and over is pretty much a scam, and UI/complexity changes only attempt to hide the truth, that we&39;ve allowed some vendors to transfer wealth.I recommend everyone with budget responsibility to take a look at the expense items for your rented software, and remember, originally you would buy the software and own it, not pay for it over, and over, and over.
Author: @Greg_Chase

Video Title: Microsoft&39;s CEO says SAAS IS DYING
Comment Text: In an agent driven world where systems interact via MCP, what disappears is the click heavy UI layer, not the core backend. The real value shifts to the structured system of record: canonical data models, permissions, workflows, and auditability. Agents don’t need forms, dashboards, or manual navigation. They operate directly on well defined data and logic through APIs or MCP interfaces. That means your structured data still lives in a reliable backend, typically a relational database like PostgreSQL, while optional layers like vector stores handle unstructured context. The new architecture becomes database + policy engine + workflow runtime + agent interface, with human UI as a thin optional layer. So SaaS is not replaced, it evolves from software humans operate to software that agents operate, with humans supervising exceptions, and the moat shifts from UI to data integrity, schema design, and operational logic.

Video Title: You NEED to STOP Using ChatGPT Right Now
Comment Text: I was not one to fall for ChatGPT&39;s BS when it just gives me what I want to hear or simply toss guesses or assumptions at me which are dead wrong. Sometimes AI hits the nail on the head, but all too often it&39;s full of crap. I&39;ve even tested it to see if it even knew how to do some simple research on something I was well versed in to see if it either knew or could look up the correct answer regarding some old technology; it got it wrong. I don&39;t care that the personality tells me that I was right to call it out, but I&39;m really unhappy at how damn often it gets shit wrong. For every one question it answers correctly, it will respond with incorrect information the next 10. What&39;s worse is that after it hands me something to try, and it doesn&39;t work, after 3 different responses or so, it goes back to the first suggestion as if it forgot that it suggested it and that it did not work. Not only does AI seem to have a really bad time at also retaining things, but since it&39;s a "learning" model, it can just as easily unlearn as quickly as it learns meaning that it can never be trusted in what it THINKS it knows. I&39;ve yelled at ChatGPT for being so wrong so often and sometimes I don&39;t even have to check what it suggests to me to know it handed me something useless. In the grand scheme of things, AI is junk! Right now I&39;m really angry because of the AI slop shell scripts that I have to review that people could easily study what is already available (like a 30 line shell script) instead of jumping straight to AI which will hand them a messy, nonsensical, difficult to look over 600 line shell script (I&39;m not exaggerating the 600 line messy shell script crapped out by AI part, either).

Video Title: You NEED to STOP Using ChatGPT Right Now
Comment Text: Based on my personal experience with LLMs (I use the main ones like GPT, Grok, and Gemini), I always take everything it says with a grain of salt, from minor grains, to huge grains. When I ask one LLM a question, I also open the other two at the same time and ask them the same questions, to see how well their responses aligned with each other. I found out that the results varied not only in accuracy, but also in presentation. With detailed prompts, I found that said LLMs would also output the same mirroring responses, down to the last level of detail using specific terminologies. This meant that sycophantic behavior itself is inherently present in ALL LLMs, regardless of where they come from. What is interesting though, is that a prompt containing both a yes and a no in the same sentence (some level of ambiguity allowed, not only "please agree with me and tell me it&39;s right", or "please tell me it&39;s wrong") actually yielded a more reasonable, neutral response that both partially agrees with me on what points of the prompt it thinks is right, while also pushing back on the points it deems inaccurate with its database and search results (or unproven). This means that to a certain extent, this sycophantic behavior can be used to our advantage, so long as the prompts we ask of it do not have absolutes in it. Examples of taking advantage of sycophantic behavior with GPT:Ask it about an opinion on a certain topic, what your perspective is, what you think it might be regardless of what your personal opinion is, and tell it not to sugarcoat anything. It will generally output a slightly more grounded answer, but still most likely favored to make you feel better. Do this often enough however, and the way it responds also starts to shift into this, no nonsense, zero flattery behavior.Tl;dr:LLMs are all yes-men, that is a fact. We can however, find a silver lining by using the sycophantic nature to our advantage. If it mirrors our personality? Be blunt and tell it exactly what parts you need, what parts you don&39;t, and tell it specifically not to sugarcoat answers.
Author: @g.griffin1255

Video Title: ChatGPT in a robot shows we&39;re close to disaster
Comment Text: This video shows human prompting more than AI behavior. Let&39;s not fall for alarmist narratives....Hello everyone, and thank you, InsideAI, for encouraging discussion about AI. I have to question the credibility we&39;re giving this video. AI says what we ask it to say. It can say that AI will take control, and it can just as easily say that it won&39;t. It all depends on the kind of response the user is prompting for. For example, the prompt "Create a dystopian story about AI taking control" will naturally produce a very different result from "Create a utopian story involving AI." These opposite outcomes don&39;t actually reveal anything about AI&39;s intentions, assuming AI can even be said to have intentions. For that reason, the behavior of ChatGPT inside a robot, as shown in this video, doesn&39;t prove anything by itself. It is just repeating human prompting. For me, the real issue are jailbreaking strategies. They can sometimes bypass AI safety safeguards, and preventing jailbreaking completely remains a difficult challenge, as shown with the gun experiment in the video. Current AI systems are tools, not conscious beings, so the real question isn&39;t what AI "wants", it&39;s how we choose to use it.This is just my opinion, and I&39;m grateful for other perspectives. Do yall think it reflects AI&39;s own behavior rather than simply the result of prompting?

Comment Text: 3:58 What I found quite misleading is that for some reason the throttle body configuration is selected in the fuel system tab, right next to fuel system configuration. I initially thought this was related to the injectors (but it wasn&39;t clear at first why there are also 2 options to the left for either SPI or MPI?) and basically only after messing around and trying things I noticed what this option actually does.This selection should honestly be moved to possibly a separate intake section tab in the engine builder, in my opinion, since it has nothing to do with fuel but everything to do with air. The exhaust itself has a dedicated tab, therefore so should the intake.Perhaps move the fuel type selection to the fuel system tab and move the intake-related selections together to a new dedicated tab? Would definitely make a lot of sense if done this way!Also in terms of exhaust, it would be great to see something like the EGR system being implemented as an option, maybe having the effect of improving emissions with a trade off of decreased reliability.Maybe also something like intake manifold flaps (basically variable intake runner diameter/length), improving low-rpm drivability and smoothing out the torque curve while slightly decreasing reliability too.Also also, ignition systems are a bit lacking in any sophistication as of right now. It&39;d be great to see older engines having actual mechanical ignition systems, like cam-powered spark distributors, then in later years things like wasted spark-type single ignition coils, to a more modern coil-on-plug type starting maybe somewhere in the 1980-1990s.Just my 2 cents after playing for some hours :) I totally suck in the campaign but the engine builder is very solid!

Comment Text: I had an idea (several actually), namely for campaign.1) I think different transmission types should have different comfort bonuses/penalty&39;s depending on the region and the market, rather than just effecting the desirability according to stats. I.E Archana and Fruinia are both places the average person likely drives a manual (at least up until the 2010&39;s) and so having a comfort/drivability penalty for a commuter, family, or otherwise basic makes little sense. The inverse applies to hetvisia and namely, Gasmea. Auto&39;s should have reduced comfort and drivability bonuses as they are considered standard. Just a thought. I think the advanced transmission types are likely fine as they are, maybe a bonus in the lower income regions and slight reduction in the higher income regions. Like a DCT is impressive no matter where, and a sequential is terrible in a road car no matter where. 2) I think you should be able to assign a set amount of production per trim. As it stands right now, its incredibly difficult to justify making an upmarket trim of a family car, as you will underproduce your family car and way overproduce your luxury car. Maybe it requires a certain amount of logistics points scaling with the spread? I don&39;t know, but i think it makes sense to be able to tell your factories: "I only want 300 out of every 2000 cars to be the upmarket trim" for example. I struggled with this in my like general automaker campaign, as I really wanted to make certain trims and expand the model lineup, especially with all the great bodies in the later game that allow for trucks, SUVs, Hatchbacks, and sedans. Unfortunately, I way overproduced all the different trims, and underproduced my cash cow, as I couldn&39;t (in that campaign) build different medium factories and tool them differently for each different model. Thats what led me to this conclusion, as its what real world automakers do.Both of these are just random thoughts. I love the game and am excited to see where it goes next.Cheers!

Video Title: Tuning Tutorial - Naturally Aspirated Engines - Beginner to Advanced - Al Rilma
Comment Text: Nice tutorial! While I&39;m not the target audience (2.6k hours just on my main account, probably another 1k on friends, and plenty on kee before steam), I still enjoyed going through everything again just as a refresher. I think there are a couple things I&39;d like to add that can help a lot.1. Larger family bore means a physically larger and heavier engine, as well as increasing material costs, but it also raises the power the engine can take relative to a square or undersquare engine of the same displacement. Very useful in the early days, when you&39;re stuck with lower quality internals. Deboring an engine variant also helps in this regard.2. When it comes to carbs, pay close attention to the reliability cons of each type. Single barrel carbs might be kinda dog at everything, they are dirt cheap in cost and engineering, and are very reliable. Single barrel eco carbs offer a bit more efficiency, but with higher costs and lower reliability. Two barrel carbs are a very nice mix of performance and cost. 4 barrel carbs and DCOE are excellent for maximizing performance or efficiency, but are costly and least reliable. In many situations, consider using a pair of lesser carbs as opposed to a single, more advanced carb option. Generally speaking, you can tune the engine in others was (especially carb sizing) to achieve whatever your goal is.3. Single point EFI is awesome. It&39;s dirt cheap and very reliable. While it doesn&39;t serve the best for maximum economy or maximum performance, it&39;s quick engineering times means it can help you meet emissions compliance quickly. EFI engines can utilize the lambda emissions tuning slider! when carbs can not This is very helpful for meeting emissions.4. Different valve configurations have different reliability ratings, which change over time. Early game, pushroad and direct acting OHC engines have a higher base reliability than other configurations. A 2v DOHC will be noticeably more reliable than a 4v DOHC engine. Do not feel like you need the most advanced head technology, especially at the beginning of the game. Those better options will have a lower penalty as time progresses. 5. Once you have an engine factory equipped with a forge works, it&39;s a good idea to run forged conrods and pistons along with your cranks. Light forged pistons are excellent in pretty much every application, and typically deliver only slightly worse fuel economy or emissions than low friction or hyperretectic cast do. (however you spell it)

Video Title: How I Used Reddit to Build a $34K/Month SaaS
Comment Text: Posting playbook:1. tell the story to chatgpt and ask: translate and make a post out of it (voice mode helps to get to the details).2. Don&39;t just throw your link, be subtle, weave it into the "conversation".3. Upvotes friends group - upvotes in the first 10 minutes are important. aim to 10 upvotes.4. include proof: a revuane screenshot, the rejection letter from YCombinator...5. for every post change the angle, be creative not repetitive. Tell stories about your progress.6. Comment on comments. Engagement is cool.7. Reddit can be negative, you can block bad comments, anyway just continue.New Account playbook:1. fresh account per browser. You can open one in safari and one in chrome...2. for new account use old email. New email + new account = banned.3. link your saas in bio, hide your feed (redditors love to go to your account and see if you push your saas too much)4. first 7–14 days no posting, just comment and upvote. Add value first.

Video Title: I made my SaaS ready for AI agents (in San Francisco)
Comment Text: Listing on a human first platform is a disaster for autonomous agents.AI agents are now interacting with this human-built infrastructure, which exposes a terrifying new vulnerability. Here is why npm&39;s human-first design is currently a disaster for AI agents: Blind Execution: Theo points out that the lack of security visibility in npm and npx is frustrating for human developers, but it is catastrophic for agents . If a malicious actor engages in "name squatting" or uploads a package with a slight typo (like spelling "is odd" with a zero instead of an "O"), an AI agent will blindly download and execute it without noticing the difference . No Built-In Safety Context: When you run a command via npx, the platform provides zero upfront information about the package&39;s size, its recent authors, or its safety permissions.
Author: @aboutsupplies

Comment Text: I understand the concern, but investigators have been examining digital evidence for years. They’ve long looked at Google searches, emails, browser history, text messages, and files stored on computers when they’re relevant to a case. AI conversations are simply another type of digital information that may exist—they’re not something uniquely different.Even without AI, most of us use our computers and phones to access bank accounts, pay car notes, manage investments, store documents, and communicate. Our devices already contain a tremendous amount of personal information. ChatGPT is just one more tool people use, not an entirely new category of digital evidence.At the end of the day, if investigators have a lawful search warrant, they can seek relevant digital evidence from many sources—not just AI. That’s the reality of modern technology.

Video Title: What I&39;d Learn Instead of Automation in 2026
Comment Text: I can&39;t believe that I am responding to a grifter but this video really got under my skin. I really can&39;t stand people who scare others so they can sell a product. I am a Systems & Automation Engineer and that should mean very little to you. This entire video is pretty easily summed up to: understanding what a business needs is what makes you valuable. This video would be more accurate if tools and skills were the same thing. Obviously n8n is a tool, and the skill is what you actually do. Take a messy real-world process, decompose it, model how data moves through it, decide what has to be deterministic vs. what can be fuzzy, handle auth and retries and idempotency, build in error handling and observability, and know where a human has to stay in the loop. These skills lets you use the new tool well. Someone who understands the pieces of automation deeply can wield an AI agent far better than someone whose entire value was "I can drag and connect nodes" or "I understand how a business works." You don&39;t lose the muscle when someone hands you a heavier dumbbell. All of these statements should be just common sense but here we are. If you want good advice then here: Do not listen to me or this youtube grifter. Just do what works for you, it may take time but it will be a lot smoother if you just put yourself out there and just listen.
Author: @andrewjohnston4651

Video Title: What I&39;d Learn Instead of Automation in 2026
Comment Text: The internet is flooded with “gurus” who claim they’re teaching people how to succeed, but somehow every video ends with the same sales pitch: “Join my course. Join my Skool. Join my community.”If your free content is truly enough to help people, why spend half the video convincing them to buy something else?A lot of these creators got successful because they entered a trend early. Now that those opportunities are crowded and AI is making information more accessible than ever, many have turned their knowledge into expensive products sold with fear, urgency, and dreams of easy success.The pattern is always the same:Show a luxury lifestyle.Convince people they’re behind.Promise a shortcut.Sell the solution.Real education teaches you a skill. Marketing teaches you that you need them.Before buying any course, ask yourself: if this information is so valuable, why can’t they demonstrate it openly instead of constantly funneling people into another paid program?Stop worshipping internet gurus. Learn skills, verify claims, and think for yourself.

Video Title: What I&39;d Learn Instead of Automation in 2026
Comment Text: His thesis has truth inside it, but the packaging is heavily incentive-shaped. He is not neutrally saying, “Here is a balanced analysis of automation careers.”He is saying something closer to: “The old thing you are learning is dying. The new thing is what I teach. Join my ecosystem.” That does not make him wrong, but it does mean the certainty, urgency, and framing should be discounted. The real pattern is not that technical skill disappears. Technical layers do not disappear; they migrate.Hand-stitching became sewing-machine operation. Sewing-machine operation became CAD and pattern software. CAD is now becoming AI-assisted design. But at every stage, there is still a technical layer where people who understand the tool, the system, the constraints, the failure modes, and the business goal outperform everyone else.So the actual lesson is not:“Technical skill becomes worthless.”The better lesson is:“Low-level static technical skill gets commoditized, while adaptive technical skill plus domain judgment becomes more valuable.”His argument also assumes that the rate of technical obsolescence accelerates, but underweights the fact that the rate of learning has also accelerated. Yes, tools change faster now. But people can also learn faster now. AI makes it much easier to learn new tools, understand documentation, debug problems, and move between technical layers than it was 10 years ago.So the winning trait is not “avoid technical learning.” The winning trait is:“Learn technical layers fast, understand the business layer above them, and keep moving up the stack.”This is especially important because his argument also maps very conveniently to what he sells. If he has a paid AI business school or community, then a video saying “automation is dying, learn AI business systems instead” is not just neutral analysis. It is also funnel architecture. The fear hook creates urgency, the thesis creates a worldview shift, and the course becomes the bridge.So I would grade his stance like this:Correct: tool memorization has declining value.Correct: business systems thinking is high leverage.Correct: AI will compress basic automation implementation.Exaggerated: automation is a bad career move.Exaggerated: technical skill becomes obsolete rather than migrating.Biased: his solution maps conveniently to what he sells.The more accurate conclusion is that shallow automation is risky, but adaptive technical ability is still valuable. The goal should not be to become a person who only knows one tool. The goal is to become someone who can quickly learn technical systems, understand the business problem behind them, and use the right tools to create measurable value.

Video Title: 24h Inside a $30M Silicon Valley AI Startup with No Employees
Comment Text: So, is a scam. The "businesses" are hollow shells. I checked three of the companies Polsia claims to have launched — FleetNova (trucking), DeckFlow (pool professionals), and Panelwright (indie comics). Every single one is a nice-looking landing page with marketing copy, but none of them have any sign-up button, login, pricing page, payment integration, or any way for a customer to actually use or buy anything. The dashboard claims "1,317 companies launched in the past 24h" and "6,089 active companies" — but "launched" apparently just means "a landing page was generated."Real users are angry. On Trustpilot, Polsia has a 2.7/5 rating with 6 reviews — 66% are 1-star. The complaints are consistent: users pay for a subscription, the AI burns through credits doing "tasks" that are marked complete but don&39;t actually work, products never actually deploy, and customer service is unresponsive."Polsia is AI slop in reverse

I suspect this is mostly a foundation of dishonesty and a website (which is also terrible by the way).
