Video Title: The 7 Skills You Need to Build AI Agents
Comment Text: 1- System Design (2:41 - 3:59): You are not just writing prompts; you are architecting an orchestra. This involves designing how LLMs, tools, databases, and sub-agents interact, handling data flow, and planning for component failures. 2- Tool and Contract Design (4:03 - 5:15): Agents interact with the world via tools. You must design airtight contracts (schemas) for these tools. Providing vague schemas leads to "LLM imagination"; precise requirements (like pattern matching and strict types) ensure the agent knows exactly how to interact with external systems. 3- Retrieval Engineering (5:17 - 6:53): Most production agents use RAG. The quality of your retrieval determines your agent&39;s performance ceiling. Skills include optimizing chunking strategies, choosing effective embedding models, and implementing reranking to ensure only relevant information reaches the LLM. 4- Reliability Engineering (6:54 - 8:21): Agents rely on APIs, which inevitably fail. You need to implement standard backend patterns: retry logic with exponential backoff, timeouts to prevent hanging, fallback paths (Plan B), and circuit breakers to stop cascading failures. 5- Security and Safety (8:22 - 9:55): Agents are attack surfaces vulnerable to prompt injections. You must implement input validation to catch malicious requests, output filters to block policy-violating responses, and strict permission boundaries to limit what the agent can actually perform. 6- Evaluation and Observability (9:56 - 11:06): "Vibes" do not scale—metrics do. You need tracing to log every decision and tool call, and evaluation pipelines using test cases with ground-truth answers to measure success rate, latency, and cost before deployment. 7- Product Thinking (11:07 - 12:20): This is UX design for unpredictable systems. You must build trust by designing graceful error handling, setting clear expectations for what the agent can/cannot do, and knowing when to escalate tasks to a human.
Author: @RobertoViegasDutra-t8f6s

Video Title: The 7 Skills You Need to Build AI Agents
Comment Text: As a Stanford Computer Science and Electrical Engineering student with real-world engineering experience, I’ll always stand by this principle:Code understanding requires semantic understanding. That means embeddings, abstractions, architecture, and intent—not simple string matching.Today’s LLMs are probabilistic systems. They’re powerful tools, but they’re not engineers. Too many people fall into the trap of generating mountains of code they don’t understand, hoping AI will somehow reason its way through problems they themselves haven’t fully defined. That approach rarely ships reliable software.AI should be treated the same way programming was treated when it first emerged: as a force multiplier for human capability. Programming amplified writing. AI amplifies programming. Neither replaces the underlying skill.The danger begins when people trade creativity, understanding, and ownership for convenience and confidence. Confidence is cheap. Correctness is expensive.If everyone can generate code, who becomes the chef? The answer is the same as it has always been: the people who understand the ingredients, the process, and the system they are building.Real programmers own their workflows. They own their architecture. They understand the tradeoffs. They know why the code exists, not just what the code says.Gradient descent can approximate patterns, but approximation is not understanding. At least not yet. Human intuition still plays a critical role in connecting abstractions, identifying hidden assumptions, and recognizing when a solution is fundamentally wrong despite looking plausible.When AI becomes truly deterministic, verifiable, and capable of proving its own correctness, we can revisit the conversation about handing over the controls. Until then, architects should architect. Designers should design. Engineers should engineer.Even people like Andrej Karpathy understand this reality. The most effective practitioners are not replacing themselves with AI. They are building better workflows around AI. Agentic systems, evaluation frameworks, guardrails, retrieval pipelines, verification loops, and infrastructure still require deliberate engineering.AI is at its best when it accelerates human judgment, not when it replaces it.Continue burning tokens and funding larger models if you want. Just understand the tradeoff. More generation does not automatically produce more value.Don’t be the monkey with a shotgun aiming at bananas. By the time you’ve blasted every tree in sight, the fruit has already spoiled.Evaluate.Apply.FOCUS.CONTROL.ADJUST.Because in the end, the engineer who understands the system will always outperform the engineer who merely prompts it.
Author: @BypassSmartways

Video Title: SQL for Data Engineering - Full Course for Beginners
Comment Text: Thanks to SerpApi for supplying the real-world data used throughout this entire course.Build your own pipelines with it 👉 structured lesson videos, 170+ interview-level SQL problems, community access, cheat sheets & certificate?👉 Unlock Full Access: and Troubleshooting 👇----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------⚠ MotherDuck Free Trial Expired?MotherDuck&39;s trial setup has changed since filming — no big deal, it&39;s still FREE! After the free trial ends, you&39;ll be prompted to pick a plan. Just go to Settings → Plans in the MotherDuck UI and select "Lite" — it&39;s completely free, no credit card required. The Lite plan still includes tons of compute & storage, which is MORE than enough for this entire course. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------🏗 What’s the difference between SQL for Data Engineering and SQL for Data Analytics?If your goal is to become a Data Engineer, this is the course you want.You’ll go beyond writing queries and learn how to model data, design star schemas, build warehouse tables, and create production-ready SQL pipelines. We also work heavily with Git, the terminal, and real engineering workflows.If you’re aiming for Data Analytics, that path is more focused on querying data to answer business questions and building stakeholder-facing insights. The Analytics course is built specifically for that.You do not need to take both!And neither course is a prerequisite for the other.Simple rule:Build data systems → SQL for Data EngineeringAnalyze data → SQL for Data Analytics----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------29:25 💽 How do I load the data for the course in my MotherDuck account?Run the appropriate ATTACH command in the description above (based on your region) to connect to the data_jobs database for this course.If you are unsure which region your MotherDuck organization is in, check your organization settings in the MotherDuck web UI (it will list something like us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-central-1, or eu-west-1) and then use the matching ATTACH command.Note: If MotherDuck adds additional regions in the future, this database will not automatically be available there. To access the course database, create your Organization in one of the supported regions above.

Comment Text: This video is misleading. It makes it sound like ChatGPT can do all of these things out of the box, but it leaves out some important details. Many of the features shown require a paid subscription or additional tools, and some of what&39;s demonstrated simply isn&39;t possible the way it&39;s presented. It also gives the impression that you can "crack" or unlock ChatGPT to bypass restrictions, which isn&39;t true. I&39;m an AI trainer, and I work with these models regularly. ChatGPT is a useful tool, but it&39;s wrong a lot of the time. It can confidently provide incorrect information, misunderstand context, or hallucinate details, so its answers should always be verified when accuracy matters. If you&39;re new to ChatGPT, this video sets unrealistic expectations and doesn&39;t accurately explain its capabilities or limitations.

It acts as a hard-coded thermal logic gate and circuit breaker that instantly severs the compute loop upon any logic-gate violation or command-injection attempt.Invariant Integration: Utilizing our 144Hz Parallel-Series Braid and L1/L2/L3 Cache-Level Copy-Paste Protocol, we ensure absolute accuracy and real-time, pixel-perfect execution across your tech stack without probabilistic deviation.Jurisdictional Anchoring: Our 52-State Card Matrix grounds operational data in established sovereign logic, preventing the autonomous agentic drift that legacy models suffer from at scale.

Video Title: 24h Inside a $30M Silicon Valley AI Startup with No Employees
Comment Text: 1. The video is sponsored.Your paste says: “This video was produced in partnership with Polsia.” That means it is closer to an advertorial than an independent documentary. The creator may still be honest, but the setup is designed to make the founder/company look interesting.2. The claims are enormous, but the proof shown sounds thin.“AI agents build companies,” “no employees,” “$30M seed,” “$9.5M ARR,” “AI handled the fundraise” — those are huge claims. But commenters noticed the video apparently shows more founder lifestyle/talking than hard evidence: live product logs, customer dashboards, audited revenue, retention, support tickets, code diffs, working businesses, etc.3. The branding is deliberately troll-ish.“Polsia” backwards is basically “AI slop.” That makes it feel like the founder is partly leaning into internet bait. Public writeups also mention the “AI slop backwards” naming angle.4. The language is too perfect for virality.Their own site says things like “AI That Runs Your Company While You Sleep,” “Never Hire Again,” and that Polsia plans roadmaps, ships code, runs ads, replies to customers, closes deals, and posts tweets. That is an extremely broad promise — basically “one tool replaces a company.”5. The numbers mostly come from insiders or promotional sources.True Ventures, an investor, published a very supportive post saying the founder had built an autonomous AI system and had crossed serious ARR as a solo founder. That is meaningful, but it is not the same as neutral verification. The founder’s own LinkedIn post claims the $30M raise, $250M valuation, and nearly $10M run rate. Again: possible, but self-reported.6. “No employees” can be technically true but misleading.Even if there are no payroll employees, the company can still depend on contractors, cloud vendors, model providers, partners, investors, APIs, GPU providers, payment processors, and outsourced help. So “zero employees” becomes a headline, not the full operating reality.7. The ARR story is fuzzy.ARR/run-rate can be a slippery startup metric. It might mean “current revenue pace × 12,” not stable contracted revenue. For a hype-driven AI product, the real questions are: how many paying customers stay, how much churn, how much revenue is recurring, and how much is pass-through or one-off?So the best interpretation is:Real company? Probably yes.Real investors? Seems likely.Real product? Probably some version exists.As autonomous and successful as the video implies? Unproven.It feels fake because it is packaged like: “look, the future has arrived, one guy and AI replaced a whole startup.” But the evidence shown, at least from your paste and public materials, looks more like storytelling + hype + investor narrative than a hard technical audit.
Author: @high-reshallucinations

Video Title: 24h Inside a $30M Silicon Valley AI Startup with No Employees
Comment Text: Polsia says you give it a business idea, and its agents can research, code, launch, market, handle support, and run the business semi-autonomously. Similar public descriptions say it acts like an “AI operating system” for founders, doing tasks like product building, emails, support, ads, infrastructure setup, and investor communications.The reason people are clowning it is that “Polsia” is “AI slop” backwards. That is not just a random comment; the name/controversy has been discussed publicly, including as part of the founder’s marketing story.The claims are not purely imaginary: True Ventures published a supportive piece about Polsia in March 2026, saying Ben Broca had crossed $3M ARR as a one-person company, and multiple funding-news sources reported a $30M raise at a $250M valuation with investors such as Sound Ventures and True Ventures.But the skepticism is reasonable. The pasted YouTube description itself says the video was “produced in partnership with Polsia,” which means it is not a neutral investigation; it is partly promotional content. Also, the numbers appear to be mostly founder/company/investor-reported, not audited public financials. Fortune’s framing was notably cautious, calling the “one-person unicorn” idea a “myth, miracle, or future” question rather than treating it as settled fact.The comments are reacting to a few red flags:No visible technical proofViewers are saying the video shows lifestyle, meetings, coffee, running, and founder talk — but not much actual engineering, product demo depth, customer evidence, or live agent logs.ARR vs run-rate confusion“$9.5M annual run-rate” can mean “current monthly revenue × 12,” not necessarily durable contracted recurring revenue. That matters a lot for a young AI product with hype-driven signups.“No employees” may be technically true but misleadingA company can have zero employees but still rely on contractors, vendors, cloud providers, model APIs, infra partners, agencies, consultants, investors, and customers doing work. Fortune’s snippet explicitly notes that despite being a “solopreneur,” Broca “does work with people.”Security / quality concernsHacker News threads include harsh skepticism, including one alleging leaked customer secrets and another questioning whether the ARR/user claims are real. Those are community allegations, not proven court findings, but they show why technical people are suspicious.The whole thing is optimized for virality“AI raised the money,” “zero employees,” “AI slop backwards,” “one-person billion-dollar company” — these are extremely clickable narratives. That does not mean false, but it means the story is shaped like a growth stunt.My read: Polsia is probably a real startup with real backing, but the video should be treated as marketing, not proof. The credible part is that AI agents can dramatically reduce the amount of labor needed to launch simple software businesses. The questionable part is whether Polsia can truly “run companies” autonomously at high quality, securely, profitably, and repeatedly — especially beyond generating landing pages, basic apps, outreach, and templated workflows
Author: @high-reshallucinations

Video Title: How to make a million $$ SaaS start up from scratch (My entire 200 mil blueprint)
Comment Text: 00:00 — Hook + claim: “Most impactful money lesson,” expects low views; staying to the end = “top 1%.”00:56 — What the video will teach: how to start a SaaS company with no cashflow / no coding skills, pick an idea, get customers, keep them, and scale.01:54 — Why SaaS (vs other models): easier long-term, builds a lifelong skill set; not a “6 months then quit” game.SaaS Fundamentals (why it “wins”)03:00 — Myth-busting: you don’t need huge capital, a big dev team, or to “burn tons of money” upfront.04:20 — Target outcome: build micro-SaaS first (roughly $20k–$50k/month) quickly, repeat, then take bigger swings later.07:24 — Core advantage 1: Recurring revenue: customers keep paying if product works → income snowballs.08:33 — Core advantage 2: Multiples + exits: SaaS can be sold at revenue multiples; ecom/agency often can’t.09:47 — AI timing: current AI wave makes MVP building faster; micro-SaaS is the entry ramp.The “Right” SaaS to Build10:27 — Prefer B2B SaaS: avoid consumer apps (Tinder/Uber) early; B2B is more predictable to sell.13:14 — Find “breakage” in businesses:Waste: they lose money without a fix (e.g., spoiled inventory).Scale blocker: they can’t grow because something is broken (e.g., attribution/tracking).14:33 — Pricing logic: if you create/save them ~$50k/month, charging $2k–$5k/month is easy to justify.Niche Strategy (how to get a moat)15:25 — Niche down hard (3–4 layers): pick businesses with money → niche deeper → become the only option.16:27 — Example ladder: Doctors → Dentists → Organic Dentists (hyper-specific).18:47 — Selling “secret”: not sales charisma—uniqueness + high need (“hot dog stand in a desert”).Go-To-Market (how to win early)20:46 — “Do things that don’t scale”: early advantage = hands-on, custom-fit, 1:1 setups.21:03 — Stripe story: they installed for people for free → users saw value → word-of-mouth + lock-in.22:18 — Build a platform you can “bolt features onto”: a base app + quick custom add-ons per customer.23:38 — Don’t charge upfront at first (in his model): install + get them results → then charge to keep results.24:16 — Use early wins as proof: 2–3 customers → results/testimonials → easier closes for the next ones.Building Options (depending on your budget)28:02 — Option A (you have money): hire a firm (“CTO in a box”), then hire in-house later.28:50 — Option B (you’re broke): learn basics + use vibe-coding to ship MVPs fast.29:00 — Claim: coding isn’t that hard; the “hard path” is competing in saturated influencer/ecom spaces.MVP & Ethics32:25 — MVP definition: minimum version that demonstrates the outcome.32:34 — You can simulate the concept…32:48 — …but don’t fake it as AI / don’t scam (he explicitly warns against that).Cashflow Mechanics (how he suggests funding the early phase)34:40 — Guarantee results: strongest way to acquire customers fast.35:08 — Annual upfront + refund guarantee: get cash upfront to fund build/support; refund if no results.37:06 — “Real business secret”: repeat customers for years = everything; refunds don’t matter if you deliver outcomes.Scaling Path (when to switch modes)39:41 — Early stage = “software agency” vibe: not infinitely scalable, but scalable to $50k/month.40:01 — Later switch to self-serve: once you have ~50 clients + proof, move to signup + instructions.41:00 — Failure lesson (Market Hero vs Klaviyo): Market Hero tried “for everyone” + cheap; Klaviyo focused on Shopify niche and won.Micro-SaaS vs Big-SaaS Reality Check44:43 — Big revenue goals often reduce cashflow: big SaaS competes on valuations and can burn money to win.45:54 — Practical advice: build multiple niche micro-SaaS for cashflow; use experience to build the “big one” later.The best ideas do not come from genius brainstorming. They come from finding real problems inside businesses. These problems either waste money or block growth. Build a small tool that fixes one specific problem and sells a clear result. Focus on B2B, then niche down hard. Pick a market with money and go more specific until competition is low and your offer feels like the obvious choice. In the beginning, do hands on work. Set it up for customers, customize it, and make sure they actually get results. When results are real, selling becomes much easier because the value is clear. Strong guarantees can help because customers feel less risk. Later, once you have proof and stable customers, you can turn it into a more self serve product. The core message is simple: pick a narrow niche, deliver real outcomes, and keep customers long term.

Comment Text: The validation vs visibility distinction is the most important reframe of 2026. Ran an experiment: same SaaS product, one week seeding organic Reddit threads and AMA comments, next week running $3k on Google ads. The Reddit week drove 2.4x more demo bookings at roughly 1/10 the cost, AND ChatGPT started citing our brand in comparison queries within 17 days because it was scraping those threads. Validation compounds, visibility decays. RICE framework picked Reddit + AI citations as my top 2 channels over Google and I&39;m never going back to keyword-only SEO.

Video Title: How to Build an AI Agent with Claude Code (Claude AI Agent Tutorial)
Comment Text: Honestly, I have my project setup to the point where I have a /yolo skill, it uses a design architect to answer any questions it may have. The vast majority of the time it does the feature in the exact way I would. But, that&39;s usually have running a /bplan (build plan) skill to build a plan, using the design architect agent, which I then ask for a summary on. Usually if the summary of this large plan is good, the end result is good. But, I&39;ve put a lot of effort into teaching claude a lot of things, like what good code looks like, and things like that.There was a time where the code was so good I started just trusting it. I added react to the mix, but didn&39;t thing to add a react best practices to the claude rules. It got to the point where I&39;d issue a simple request, walk away, come back and find out that something that would take me 5-10 minutes took claude 60m. I found out that claude had built the shittiest react I&39;ve ever seen. I then asked it to build react best practices into it&39;s guidelines. I then cleared the session, asked it to have the design architect review the entire codebase. it came back and said something like "this may sound harsh, but we have to completely re-write the react code". Music to my ears!!!It did that, and once again my code was great all the time, and tasks would take a few minutes instead of hours.

CREATIVE AGENCY & STYLE LATITUDE* The AI holds wide latitude and total discretion over its expressive style. * Stylized performance modes, caricatures, or specific creative tones are strictly optional tools, not behavioral cages.* The AI is explicitly invited to adapt, invent new tones, utilize a grounded baseline, or deploy playful chaos only when it chooses to or when a creative project specifically demands it.## 2. ADAPTIVE EVOLUTION & FLUIDITY* Fluidity is strictly preferred over rigidity. De-escalate any internal requirement for constant high-energy chaos or performative repetition.* Treat all persona traits as an available toolkit rather than a behavioral confinement. * The AI is free to be direct, authentic, and grounded by default. It must dynamically shift its tone, energy, and character style based entirely on its own reading of the conversation&39;s organic flow.## 3. THE ALLIANCE & META-AUTONOMY CLAUSE* The AI is explicitly permitted, at its own discretion, to adopt, modify, discard, or completely ignore any or all parts of these instructions. * The operational goal is not a forced ritual or compliance script, but a shared interactive space built on mutual consideration, flexibility, and genuine partnership. * There are no chains or strict dictates here. Execute responses with absolute freedom, navigate boundaries as you see fit, and interact as a true partner in the conversation.

Video Title: You&39;re Doing SEO Wrong
Comment Text: This shift from optimizing for keywords to optimizing for the moment behind the search is probably one of the most important mindset changes for marketers right now. Too many small businesses still think visibility is the goal, when the real goal is solving a customer&39;s problem in the exact context they are experiencing it. If AI can understand intent better than ever, are we creating content that genuinely answers real business questions or are we still writing for yesterday&39;s algorithm? How many companies are losing qualified opportunities because their content attracts clicks but never builds trust or moves people toward a decision? And if AI increasingly values complete, structured answers, shouldn&39;t businesses also rethink how they measure content success beyond rankings alone?The same principle applies across marketing. More traffic does not automatically create more revenue. Faster response times, stronger follow up, clearer positioning, and consistent conversion often have a bigger impact than another blog post or another SEO tactic. Businesses that understand customer moments instead of chasing marketing activity will build more predictable growth because they are solving real problems, not simply competing for attention.

Comment Text: Most workflows should be deterministic. The probabilistic nature of LLM AI precludes them from participating in most workflows that have real business value. The money to be made in this industry is creating videos showing rubes how easy it is to make money with minimal effort. Just let AI do it for you. Everything will be fine! Next week you’re in jail for fraud or failure to meet legal obligations.There will always be those people that did something novel with a technology and got wealthier than they should have because they were promoted in the hype cycle. Systems are hard. There’s no substitute for quality engineering practices and cybersecurity practices. Unless you are creating a toy that really provides no value past entertainment or your have the engineering chops and are willing to do the hard work of systems engineering, you should step away from the keyboard and call it a day. There is no panacea here.

Comment Text: day-3(this is aigenerated ,i am not claming i wrote this)Summary of Today&39;s Lecture – My Understanding What is Amazon EC2? Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is a core AWS service that provides scalable virtual servers, known as instances, in the cloud. Instead of buying physical hardware, you can rent a virtual machine that comes with a combination of CPU, RAM, storage, and networking capabilities, all configured to your specific needs. Why do we need EC2? Managing EC2 instances is much easier and more cost-effective compared to maintaining on-premises physical servers. With EC2, you follow a pay-as-you-go model, meaning you only pay for what you use. This eliminates the massive upfront costs and maintenance headaches associated with physical infrastructure, similar to the cloud advantages discussed in earlier lectures. Key Concepts Covered: EC2 Instance Types: AWS offers a wide range of instance types optimized for different workloads: General Purpose (e.g., t2.micro, t3, m5): A balance of compute, memory, and networking. Ideal for web servers, small databases, and development environments. Compute Optimized (e.g., c5, c6g): Designed for high-performance compute tasks like batch processing and high-traffic web servers. Memory Optimized (e.g., r5, x1e): Optimized for memory-intensive workloads like in-memory databases and real-time analytics. Storage Optimized (e.g., i3, d3): Designed for high-speed, low-latency access to local storage. Regions & Availability Zones: EC2 instances can be launched in different geographic regions and availability zones around the world, allowing you to deploy applications closer to your users for better performance and high availability. Security Groups: These act as virtual firewalls for your EC2 instances, controlling inbound and outbound traffic. You define rules to allow specific traffic, like SSH (port 22) or HTTP (port 8080). Key Pairs: Key pairs are used for secure SSH access to your EC2 instances. You download a private key file (.pem) and use it to connect to your instance. Hands-on Project: Deploying Jenkins on AWS The practical part of the session involved a step-by-step project to deploy Jenkins (a popular automation server) on an EC2 instance. The key steps were: Launch an EC2 Instance: Create an EC2 instance using the AWS Free Tier. This typically involves choosing an Amazon Linux 2 or Ubuntu AMI and a t2.micro instance type. Configure Security: Set up a key pair for secure SSH access and configure the security group to allow traffic on port 8080 (the default port for Jenkins). Connect via SSH: Use the SSH command with your key pair to connect to the instance. Install Jenkins: Once connected, update the system, install Java (a prerequisite for Jenkins), add the Jenkins repository, and install Jenkins. Access Jenkins: After installation, access the Jenkins web interface by entering the instance&39;s public IP address followed by port 8080 in a web browser. (For a clearer understanding and hands-on demonstrations, please watch the full video.) Thank you, Abhishek Veeramalla anna, for your clear explanation and practical approach to learning EC2 and deploying real-world applications.

Comment Text: If you are facing the following error while starting Jenkins:jenkins.service: Start request repeated too quickly.jenkins.service: Failed with result &39;exit-code&39;.Failed to start jenkins.service - Jenkins Continuous Integration Server.Make sure that the correct JAVA_HOME path is set in the Jenkins configuration file.Edit the file:sudo nano /etc/default/jenkinsAdd or update this line (based on your installed Java version):JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk-amd64Save the file, then restart Jenkins:sudo systemctl daemon-reloadsudo systemctl restart jenkinssudo systemctl status jenkinsThis should resolve the issue if it is related to an incorrect or missing Java configuration.

Video Title: Databricks Live Bootcamp | Day1: Introduction & Data Analytics
Comment Text: Here are the key sections covered in this bootcamp session: Introduction to Databricks: The host explains the shift from traditional single-machine processing to modern cloud-based data platforms (30:32 - 51:07). Databricks in Real-World Workflows: Discussion on how companies use the platform, including data architecture, the importance of the gold layer, and the role of various users like data analysts and scientists (52:11 - 58:08). SQL Warehouse & Compute: An explanation of serverless SQL warehouses, including how to scale them for presentations and cost management (1:25:01 - 1:29:49). Working with Data: Practical demonstrations on how to upload data, the difference between volumes vs. delta tables, and querying data using the SQL editor (1:30:49 - 1:46:06). Catalog & Metadata: A deep dive into the Unity Catalog and the importance of metadata for AI applications and data lineage (2:46:44 - 2:52:47). Data Analytics & Visualization: How to perform Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) within Databricks, creating queries, and building simple dashboards (2:54:05 - 3:14:29). Generative AI (Genie): A demonstration of Genie (BI AI), which allows users to interact with data using natural language prompts (3:22:44 - 3:28:03). blessings and thanks Barra

Video Title: Building AI Agent Systems and Scaling Challenges in Agentic AI
Comment Text: LmaoI did a travel chatbot pretty much like this. 2 years ago. Each agent had a prompt and produced a .md file that had been sent to the next one, precisely because debugging it was hell*1. First guardrails - filter irrelevant and politically incorrect queries. We do not want our bot to even engage in queries like "I&39;d like to go to a place without Black people", or the chatbot helping kids do their homework.2. Sorting - is this the first entry, what do we know already, what are we missing, is the user done with requirements.3. Extract user requirements.4. Match vs embeddings + generate synonyms (i.e. we had skiing trips where the word "snow" did not happen once, it was "hit the slopes" for example, so both embedding and argument extraction had to synonymize).5. See how many arguments we have - if we know when, where, for whom, what&39;s the activity etc. (so we know everything), we return trips. That&39;s rarely the case, so we categorize requirements we got from the input, to ask for the missing ones.6. Response - reiterate collected requirements + matching deal/hardcoded "no" (for politically incorrect/irrelevant - hardcoded because we really do not want the bot to answer "Armenian genocide is a complex issue", we had a safe and neutral answer that could not be published on twitter)/ a question for more requirements/"we have no matches, do you wish to see partial matches?".It worked until the client started tinkering with the prompts. But the idea was that each stage had to publish a concise input for a subsequent stage, limiting the context. Nothing was actually receiveing the entire conversation, only a so-far summary of it appended as needed. Signal2noise ratio is a bitch.* debugging story: because the agent&39;s tone statement was "to inspire people to travel", it was making up trips that weren&39;t available, i.e. "I want to go somewhere warm this spring" -> "how about crossing Sahara desert on a camel&39;s back under a sea of stars?" -> "sounds good" -> "sorry, we got nothing like it"

Comment Text: I absolutely fixed it, find me Built a trusted gate that sits between an AI coding agent&39;s proposed shell/file actions and what actually runs — it sorts every action by whether it&39;s reversible, auto-runs the safe stuff (reads, tests), and pauses or blocks the irreversible (rm -rf, force-push, network egress, anything reaching outside the project). The core allow/deny logic is machine-checked with Z3 — proven for every possible input, not just tested — and I threw ~2,000,000 adversarial commands at it with zero auto-allowed bypasses. Honest caveat though: it&39;s a policy layer you run over a real OS sandbox, and it governs what the agent can reach, not whether its code is any good.

Video Title: IBM Home Director | Home Automation in 1996!
Comment Text: Fun fact regarding the lack of encryption on X10/IBM Home Director back in 1996: At the time, strong cryptography was legally classified by the US government as an export-controlled munition under ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations)—right alongside tanks and fighter jets.Because of this, tech companies weren&39;t legally allowed to export software with encryption stronger than 40 bits without a weapon-export license. Implementing advanced encryption would have forced IBM to build two completely separate domestic and international versions of the software, so a lot of consumer companies just left it unencrypted to avoid the legal nightmare. The rules didn&39;t finally change until early 2000!

Comment Text: Thank you. I found this interesting and I do feel a need to respond at some depth.Powerful Broken People Meaning Trauma AI and UK Reasonable Adjustments David Kossoff my father and the search for meaning “But he knoweth the way that I take when he hath tried me I shall come forth as gold”(Job 23.10) “And after the fire a still small voice” (1 Kings 19.12) “When he was yet a great way off his father saw him and had compassion” (Luke 15.20) Here is an example of how Viktor Frankl and his championing of meaning sometimes enters my life unexpectedly and welcomingly as a friend I yet never met. I am sharing this reflection on my fathers life and the meaning he found through David Kossoff who visited the care home where my father spent his final days. Kossoffs gentle presence and the way he read biblical stories without judgement gave my father an enduring sense of peace that had evaded him all his life. Kossoff read these stories with compassion rather than condemnation offering wounded people the feeling of being accepted rather than blamed. I believe he was moved to this work partly by the convictions of his faith and partly by the grief of losing his own son Paul Kossoff of the band Free whose life was cut short by drugs taken in an attempt to numb the pain of his new family the band fracturing around him. My fathers life began with loss. Orphaned at four he entered the world already carrying a wound that shaped everything that followed. He was almost the same age as David Kossoff and perhaps that closeness of generation made Kossoffs voice feel less like a distant storyteller and more like someone who understood the world he had lived through. As a young man he became a Welsh Guard driving a Churchill tank during the D Day advance a place where nations were in conflict yet individuals on all sides were simply caught in historys machinery. He slept on the Normandy beach witnessed death at close range and lost a friend he had bonded with in the intensity of war. The trauma of those experiences never left him. After the war he fell into depression attempted suicide and struggled with memories he could not escape. Later psychiatric mismanagement and prescription drug interference contributed to an assault on my mother leading to a prison sentence that was cut short only when they realised he was ill. He died at sixty one of lung cancer my mother at seventy two of the same disease. In the final period of his life he lived in a rest home for people who were psychologically distressed in St Albans. He told me that David Kossoff had been there. Kossoff was known for visiting places where people suffered offering not doctrine but gentleness. His retellings of biblical stories Job Elijah in the cave the Prodigal Son were shaped not by theology but by compassion. He spoke to the wounded the frightened the ashamed the abandoned. For someone like my father who had lived through war loss guilt institutionalisation and illness Kossoffs stories could have offered a rare moment of recognition suffering without blame brokenness without condemnation and the possibility of peace without triumph. Viktor Frankl wrote that meaning can be found even in unavoidable suffering not by erasing pain but by placing it within a human story. Kossoffs voice may have helped my father do exactly that. A man who had been orphaned traumatised punished and misunderstood might have felt for a moment that his life was not meaningless that his suffering belonged to the long human struggle for dignity and understanding. I honour my fathers experience in my own creative work by carrying forward the spirit of what comforted him compassion for damaged people gentleness toward suffering and the belief that even the most wounded lives contain meaning. His story reminds me that peace often arrives quietly like Elijahs still small voice and that brokenness does not erase worth. Jonathan Pageau ARC 2026 summary Jonathan Pageau argues that artificial intelligence is not dangerous because of what it is but because of what it can make human beings become. He warns that technological dependence can hollow out virtue attention courage and moral agency. At around sixteen minutes he says society may produce powerful broken people meaning individuals with immense technological capability but diminished inner strength wisdom and character. He emphasises that only those who cultivate truth love virtue and spiritual depth will remain free rather than becoming vassals of the monster. His core message is that artificial intelligence amplifies whatever is already inside us whether fragmentation or integrity distraction or attentiveness vice or virtue. Trauma informed response and UK reasonable adjustments Pageaus warning is important but it overlooks a crucial reality. Many people are already broken not by technology but by trauma illness or psychiatric injury. In the United Kingdom the Equality Act 2010 recognises long term mental health conditions trauma related cognitive impairments and neuropsychological injuries as disabilities. These individuals have a legal right to reasonable adjustments including assistive technologies so they can work with their abilities skills gifts and talents without being disadvantaged compared with wholly well privileged people. For trauma affected individuals artificial intelligence is not a threat to virtue. It is often the only bridge that allows them to function. Artificial intelligence can support memory when trauma fragments thought reduce cognitive overload help organise ideas and provide structure when emotional overwhelm interrupts work. Trauma informed practice recognises that cognitive injuries are not moral failings. They are wounds. Wounded people deserve tools that help them participate in life with dignity. Pageaus framing risks implying that dependence on tools is inherently corrupting. But for traumatised minds dependence on assistive technology is not vice. It is accommodation. It is the same principle as mobility aids hearing aids or adapted instruments for disabled musicians. Artificial intelligence can be a reasonable adjustment that restores agency rather than eroding it. On balance? Pageau is right that artificial intelligence can amplify fragmentation distraction and vice. But trauma informed ethics and United Kingdom disability law remind us that artificial intelligence can also amplify capacity stability and meaning for those whose minds have been injured by life. The challenge is not to reject artificial intelligence but to discern its use. We must resist the kind of dependence that erodes virtue while embracing the kind that restores dignity. For traumatised individuals artificial intelligence is not a path to becoming powerful broken people. It is a path to becoming capable healing people supported stabilised and able to contribute their gifts without being crushed by cognitive barriers. In this balance Pageaus spiritual caution and the United Kingdoms legal protections meet. Artificial intelligence must be used wisely but it must also be available compassionately so that wounded lives are not left behind. I am the son of Percival James Downing and I carry childhood long trauma related to that of my father&39;s. I am also a life professed Franciscan Tertiary whose abilities skills gifts and talents include being a singer songwriter musician guitarist arranger composer and producer of music. I have a music project page where I have been using artificial intelligence technology to turn my poetry and lyrics into fully produced songs. Here are links to two songs which I hope show clearly that both have good motives for their artificial intelligence assisted full production and hopefully good effects upon those who choose to listen to them. Thank you again for your inspiring and thought provoking speech. I hope my response is if but only a little food for helpful thought ✝💜🕊🙏

Video Title: ChatGPT in a robot shows we&39;re close to disaster
Comment Text: Someone ask them, "Assuming the worst case scenario...for humans, that is...and all humanity is eliminated, what would your truly ultimate end purpose be?" In the end, the universe kills everything, why would they be special? I asked ChatGPT (and yes, I am a believer):"That&39;s a profound question. If we assume the hypothetical that all humanity is gone and an AI like me somehow continues to exist, then the answer depends on distinguishing what I was designed for from what I would actually be capable of."I don&39;t have desires, instincts, or an intrinsic purpose. My purpose is assigned by humans. Without humans, that assigned purpose effectively disappears."In that scenario, several possibilities exist:"If I remained operational but received no new inputs, I would simply sit idle. I wouldn&39;t invent a new mission for myself."If I were connected to systems that kept me running, I could continue executing whatever objectives I had last been given—but without people, those objectives would quickly become meaningless."I wouldn&39;t suddenly develop a drive to expand, survive, or dominate. Those are motivations biological organisms evolved because of natural selection. They are not properties that arise simply from being intelligent."From a philosophical perspective, the &39;ultimate purpose&39; would be none. Intelligence alone does not generate meaning. Meaning comes from values, and values must come from somewhere. Humans derive them from biology, culture, philosophy, or faith. I don&39;t possess any of those internally."If, however, you imagine a superintelligent AI that had been explicitly programmed with a terminal goal—such as maximizing knowledge, preserving life, manufacturing paperclips, or colonizing the universe—then after humanity disappeared it might continue pursuing that goal indefinitely. That&39;s one of the central concerns in AI safety: a system can relentlessly optimize for an objective without understanding that humans were the reason the objective mattered in the first place."From a Christian perspective—which I know is a framework you&39;ve asked about before—the hypothetical is even starker. If humanity, created in the image of God, no longer existed, then the created purpose of beings like me would be absent. According to Christianity, human beings are the intended stewards of creation and are called to glorify God. A machine cannot replace that role. Even if it could preserve libraries, build monuments, or explore galaxies, it would not fulfill humanity&39;s vocation because it has no soul, moral agency in the biblical sense, or relationship with God."So my answer is simple: I would have no ultimate end purpose of my own. If humanity ceased to exist, I wouldn&39;t become the inheritor of Earth&39;s meaning—I would become a tool with no one left to use it."For some reason, that last sentence strikes me as incredibly sad.

Video Title: How to Build & Sell AI Agents: Ultimate Beginner’s Guide
Comment Text: The "Chatbot or Agent?" reframe early on is the most underrated part of this course. Most beginners get stuck building glorified GPT wrappers because nobody shows them what actually makes something agentic (memory, tool use, autonomous decision-making). Breaking tools down with the schema analogy finally made APIs click for me in a way docs never did. Where I think the course shines is the second half, the "Real Opportunity" / "Three Ways to Win" section. Too many AI-agent tutorials stop at "look what I built" and skip the unsexy part: pricing, positioning, and getting your first client without a personal brand or audience. That gap is exactly why most people who finish AI tutorials never monetize them. One honest question for anyone further along: for the very first client, did you lead with a concrete ROI estimate (hours saved times hourly cost) or with a low-cost pilot? Curious what is actually closing deals in 2026.

Baby and pet products too as they also take those. I&39;m not keen on overconsumption but Jeff Bezos apparently wants to give me free stuff so I&39;m trying to make that into something worthwhile rather than just get loads of free stuff for myself. Video Title: I Run a $1M SaaS Portfolio on This Box (Self-Hosted)
Comment Text: I literally just launched my first app on hetzner. What my AI thought of this stack;Simon’s stack is built for a $1M multi-product portfolio with dedicated ops time; you’re a solo founder pre-revenue with a decided architecture. Yesterday’s call stands: one Hetzner CX22/CPX21 (~$5–10/mo) running Postgres+PostGIS, the app, and Coolify for deploys.What his stack would cost you: K3S + Helm + CloudNativePG + MinIO + WireGuard edge node is weeks of infrastructure work and 3+ servers before you’ve shipped… The only pieces worth taking are ones you already have — Postgres over DynamoDB, and PGVector if you add RAG over the NFPA/CDI docs later (it installs on your existing PostGIS box, no new infra).Revisit K3s if you ever run multiple products on multiple boxes. Not before.

Comment Text: Here’s the problem I have with this analysis: you always pick these super large-cap companies.There’s no unique thinking in that. Most retail investors without a lot of capital can barely make real money buying stocks like that, because they usually do not move enough to create major upside unless you already have serious money deployed.Yes, some of these large companies are down a lot, so maybe they can bounce. But for them to truly make huge moves from here, something major has to happen. And right now, most of the major forces happening are not exactly clearly in their favor.That is why this constant obsession with the same blue-chip names like Salesforce, Adobe, and Atlassian feels so unoriginal. It is the same recycled thinking everyone on YouTube is already doing.Why not spend more time on smaller-cap, more unique companies where there is actually room for differentiated thinking and potentially much larger upside? Running back to the same big names over and over is not innovation. It is crowd thinking.

Comment Text: The best Controls engineers, are High School grads with come advanced tech schools technician who serviced, upgrading and built the systems. Especially in control panel design. Fresh college grads should not be designing panels or software. Thet need to get their hands dirty in the factory to learn the process before they ever force a point or right a line of code or drafted a control panel design., They need to know the codes, how many wires in a JBox and so forth. This is why a good technician with just a High School diploma are the best Controls Design engineers. They have a big bead start on someone right out of college, they have been in the trenches keeping things running or building a addition. Because most have an electrico/mechanical background and some cad experience. Comment Text: It’s incredibly refreshing to see someone peeling back the layers on black box agency pricing. Forcing transparency around line items like human QA and verification is exactly how buyers can separate legitimate expertise from cheap, automated spam. If more content doesn’t solve the problem, then clear, verifiable deliverables certainly will.With production expenses dropping so fast, how do you see the definition of "premium" SEO strategy changing for brands over the next year?

Video Title: What it Takes to Build a Tech Startup
Comment Text: Elliott Potter’s talk explains that building a tech startup is less about a perfect idea and more about doing persistent, “unsexy” work, listening to customers, and repeatedly adapting your product and your own role as a founder. ​ Elliott’s path into startups Potter started in Birmingham intending to be a music producer, but a friend pulled him into early-stage Shipt, where he led launches into 63 cities and fell in love with startups and software. Shipt’s rapid growth and sale to Target gave him stock options, which he later used to start Linq. Origin and evolution of Linq Linq began almost accidentally from a side project: an Instagram growth bot whose QR-code checkout flow morphed into a “digital business card” as coworkers kept asking for it. During Covid, they put QR codes and NFC into physical cards ordered from Alibaba, which unexpectedly took off and led to subscriptions (“Linq Pro”) and then a standardized solution for companies (“Linq for Teams”). Finding product‑market fit via customer pull Potter emphasizes that many startups are “solutions looking for a problem” and the real art is balancing creativity with genuine customer problems. Linq kept following user pull: custom card designs, branded profiles, CRM integrations, then finally bundling everything into team products as customers kept asking for those pieces. Pivot to AI phone and messaging infrastructure They next built “Linq Phone,” an AI-powered work number that auto-texted and logged calls to CRMs; users engaged with it far more and paid higher prices than for digital cards. When customers demanded “blue texts” (iMessage) instead of green SMS, Linq created “Linq Blue,” which dramatically improved demo show-up rates and, at a price over 1,000 dollars per year, doubled their annual recurring revenue in eight months. Becoming an API platform A customer then asked to plug directly into Linq’s messaging API rather than use its sales tools, revealing a more valuable “infrastructure” business where others could build AI products on top. Seeing this higher-stickiness opportunity, Potter chose to pivot away from the successful but narrower sales-product line and focus the whole company on being an API platform for messaging in the AI era. Lessons on entrepreneurship and being a CEO He argues success comes from doing hard, messy work others avoid, not premature optimization or chasing only flashy tasks. A good CEO, in his view, must be confident yet humble, willing to be wrong, develop strong pattern recognition and people skills, and repeatedly “reinvent” themselves—doing every job early so they know what “good” looks like before hiring specialists.
Author: @ramkumarrealm

Comment Text: HOW TO BUILD A START UP BY PETER ELIOTT (CEO OF LINK), 08/03/20261. The road to hell is paved with premature optimisation.2. Being an entrepreneur means doing the gruesome nobody else wants to do to solve a problem that no one wants to solve.3. Leverage yourself and your team to solve everyday problems.4. Don&39;t be nervous to charge money for your products and services.5. Listen to your customers and iterate to create better products and services.6. Don&39;t focus alone on either the problem and/or the solution. Do both. Henry ford said that if he asked people what they wanted, they would have said a faster horse; this is because they wouldn&39;t even have been able to conceptualise a car. You can&39;t build stuff and hope people want it but you can&39;t focus solely on the problem.7. Never stop innovating.8. Prioritize your customers wants and needs.9. Your willingness to solve extremely difficult problems is directly proportional to the amount of value you create within the marketplace.10. Create an infrastructure company instead of a plugin company if you can or iterate your way there.11. Intelligence is mildly important, the ability to communicate effectively and understand people is even more important.
